Annual OKU Double Deduction Summary report lists each OKU employee with their total remuneration, eligible amount, and computed double deduction for the year of assessment
Report notes that double deduction extended to YA2030 per Budget 2026 and identifies applicable YA range
technicalNotes: Income Tax Act 1967: Section 34(6)(n) â€” additional deduction on remuneration paid to disabled employees
Eligible cash remuneration: basic salary, OT, bonus, commission, monthly fixed allowances â€” excludes non-cash BIK
Double deduction cap: RM48,000 per OKU employee per year of assessment
Monthly salary cap for eligibility: RM4,000/month (employee earning above this does not qualify)
Budget 2026 extended double deduction for hiring OKU employees; also extends to care workers training under KPWKM programmes
Employer records claim in corporate tax Form C â€” payroll provides the supporting schedule

userStories.title: Implement Salary Sacrifice Arrangement PCB and EPF Contribution Base Reduction
userStories.description: Salary sacrifice (also called salary packaging) is a contractual arrangement where an employee foregoes part of their cash salary in exchange for employer-provided non-cash benefits (e.g., company car, laptop, gym membership, child care). For PCB purposes, the sacrificed salary amount is excluded from gross employment income only if the benefit received is a LHDN-approved BIK or perquisite exemption. For EPF, contributions must be computed on the agreed reduced salary (post-sacrifice), not the original salary. Payroll must correctly model this two-step adjustment to avoid over-deducting PCB and EPF.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Sacrifice salary component type reduces gross salary for both PCB and EPF contribution base calculation
System validates that the sacrificed amount corresponds to a mapped benefit component (BIK or exempt perquisite)
If the benefit received is a taxable BIK (not exempt), the BIK prescribed value is added back to PCB chargeable income
EPF monthly contribution for both employer and employee is computed on post-sacrifice gross salary
EA Form shows pre-sacrifice gross in aggregate income and shows sacrifice deduction and corresponding BIK separately
Report lists all salary sacrifice arrangements per employee with benefit type, sacrifice amount, and PCB/EPF impact
technicalNotes: LHDN Public Ruling 2/2013 on Employment Income covers salary sacrifice and BIK interaction
EPF contributions must be on 'wages' as defined in EPF Act â€” post-sacrifice amount counts as wages when sacrifice is validly documented
Key risk: if the sacrifice is not for an approved BIK or perquisite, the sacrificed amount remains taxable employment income
Common use cases: car parking, company car, petrol, laptop, childcare subsidy by employer
Distinguish from 'voluntary' EPF contributions â€” salary sacrifice reduces the contribution base; voluntary contributions top it up
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module
Implement Perquisite Exemption Thresholds on Salary Components

userStories.title: Enforce Director Dividend Distribution PCB Exclusion to Prevent Incorrect PCB Deduction
userStories.description: Company directors who are also shareholders may receive dividend distributions from the company in addition to their director fee or salary. Under Section 25 of the Income Tax Act 1967, dividends are investment income (not employment income) and must NOT be subject to PCB deduction by the employer. The payroll system currently handles director fee vs director salary distinction, but lacks a classification guard to prevent payroll administrators from incorrectly routing dividend payments through the Salary Slip module, which would trigger an erroneous PCB deduction.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Dividend Distribution' salary component type is introduced and classified as non-employment income
When a dividend component is included in any Salary Slip, system raises a hard-block validation: 'Dividend distributions are not employment income â€” process via Journal Entry, not Salary Slip'
EA Form excludes any amount tagged as dividend distribution from Section A employment income totals
Director employee profile shows total dividend distributions for the year separately from salary/fee for reference (not for PCB)
System guide/tooltip explains correct accounting entry for dividend distributions vs director fee/salary
If dividend component is accidentally submitted on a Salary Slip, system logs a compliance warning and requires manual override with reason
technicalNotes: ITA 1967 Section 25: definition of employment income excludes dividends
Dividends are taxable under Schedule 7 (investment income) when received by individual shareholders â€” not subject to employer PCB withholding
Single-tier dividend system: company pays dividends from after-tax profits; recipient does not need to pay further tax
Director fee (Section 13 employment income) IS subject to PCB; dividend is NOT â€” payroll must clearly separate these
Distinct from 'Distinguish director fee vs director salary at invoice level' story â€” this covers dividend exclusion, not fee vs salary
dependencies: Distinguish director fee vs director salary at invoice level

userStories.title: Calculate and Record EPF Late Contribution Interest Penalty per KWSP Act
userStories.description: Under the Employees Provident Fund Act 1991, employers who remit EPF contributions after the monthly payment deadline (15th of the following month, or last working day if 15th is a holiday) are liable to pay dividends/interest on late contributions at 1% per month or RM5 (whichever is higher) until full payment. The payroll system currently tracks EPF contribution schedules but does not compute or record late payment penalties, which are frequently incurred by SMEs and must be reconciled before the annual EPF audit.
acceptanceCriteria: EPF Contribution Batch record has a Payment Date field capturing the actual remittance date
When Payment Date is after the statutory deadline, system computes penalty: 1% of unpaid amount per month (pro-rated daily) or RM5 minimum
Penalty amount is displayed on the EPF Contribution Batch record and flagged for payment in the next cycle
Monthly EPF reconciliation report shows: due date, actual payment date, days late, penalty amount per batch
System alerts the payroll administrator on the 13th of each month if the current month's EPF batch has not been marked as submitted
Annual late payment penalty summary is generated per year for corporate finance reporting
technicalNotes: EPF Act 1991 Section 45: late contribution penalty at 1% per month or RM5 minimum (whichever is higher)
Payment deadline: 15th of the month following the salary month; adjusted to previous business day if 15th is a weekend/holiday
KWSP may issue a penalty notice (Notice 1A) for persistent late payment; employer may appeal within 14 days
Penalty is calculated on total outstanding amount (employer + employee share) per contribution batch
Distinct from SOCSO late payment penalty (6% per annum, minimum RM5 per month)
dependencies: Generate EPF Borang A monthly contribution schedule

userStories.title: Handle PERKESO Temporary Disablement Benefit (TDB) Salary Offset During Work Injury Leave
userStories.description: When an employee sustains a work-related injury covered by SOCSO Employment Injury Scheme, PERKESO pays a Temporary Disablement Benefit (TDB) at 80% of the employee's insured daily wage for each day of certified absence. During TDB-covered leave, the employer is typically NOT required to pay full salary (the PERKESO benefit substitutes it). However, many employers continue paying full salary and must reconcile the PERKESO benefit received. Payroll must track TDB periods, apply the correct salary treatment, and avoid double-payment of SOCSO and employer salary simultaneously.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee Leave record has a 'SOCSO Work Injury Leave' type linked to PERKESO TDB claim number
During SOCSO injury leave, payroll salary calculation shows PERKESO TDB daily rate (80% of insured daily wage, capped at SOCSO contribution ceiling) as an offset against employer salary obligation
Employer salary payment during TDB period is configurable: Full Pay (employer tops up), Partial Pay, or No Pay (PERKESO-only)
EA Form correctly classifies PERKESO TDB receipts (not employment income) â€” employer payments during injury leave are shown as employment income
TDB claim tracking shows: injury date, TDB start date, end date, total TDB days, daily rate, total PERKESO amount expected
Warning if employee is being paid full employer salary AND TDB concurrently without a top-up arrangement documented
technicalNotes: SOCSO Employment Injury Scheme: TDB rate = 80% of insured daily wage rate (IDR)
TDB paid by PERKESO directly to employee, not through employer â€” employer's obligation is reduced accordingly
Insured Daily Rate cap based on SOCSO contribution table (wage ceiling RM6,000/month effective Oct 2024)
Employment Act S.60F: sick leave entitlement runs concurrently with TDB â€” but TDB replaces pay obligation
PERKESO TDB claim reference to be stored on the injury leave record for reconciliation
Relevant PERKESO forms: Borang 34 (accident notification, 48-hour deadline) â€” link to existing story
dependencies: Generate PERKESO Borang 34 Accident Notification Within 48-Hour Statutory Deadline

userStories.title: Record FWCMS Monthly Levy Payment Receipt Reference on Foreign Employee Record
userStories.description: After paying the Foreign Worker Levy via the FWCMS portal (fwcms.gov.my), employers receive a payment receipt with a unique reference number per foreign worker per month. The existing PRD covers levy tracking and payment deadline alerts, but does not capture the post-payment receipt reference. MOHR and Immigration Department audits require employers to produce evidence of levy payment for each foreign worker â€” the receipt reference number is the primary evidence. This story closes the audit trail loop by storing the FWCMS receipt reference on the foreign employee's levy payment record.
acceptanceCriteria: Foreign Worker Levy Payment record has a FWCMS Receipt Reference Number field
After levy payment is made on FWCMS portal, HR records the receipt reference and payment date on the employee's levy record
Monthly levy status per foreign employee shows: expected levy amount, payment date, FWCMS receipt reference, and payment confirmation flag
Annual audit export generates a report of all FWCMS payments per foreign employee with receipt references for Immigration/MOHR inspection
Alert is raised for any foreign employee levy month where payment date has passed but FWCMS receipt reference has not been recorded
Batch import of FWCMS receipt references is available (CSV/Excel upload) for employers with many foreign workers
technicalNotes: FWCMS portal: fwcms.gov.my â€” managed by Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA)
Foreign Worker Levy payment deadline: 10th of each month (separate from EPF/SOCSO/EIS deadline of 15th)
Receipt reference format from FWCMS portal to be confirmed; typically an alphanumeric transaction ID
Multi-Tier Levy Model (MTLM): levy rate varies by sector, worker nationality, and tier â€” reference existing levy tracking story for rate calculation
Receipt reference must be retained for at least 7 years per MOHR record-keeping requirements
dependencies: Implement Foreign Worker Levy Tracking (FWCMS / Multi-Tier Levy Model)
FWCMS Foreign Worker Levy: 10th-of-Month Payment Deadline Alert Separate from EPF/SOCSO

userStories.title: PDPA 2024 DPO Appointment Registry and 72-Hour Payroll Data Breach Notification Workflow
userStories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024, effective 1 June 2025, requires employers processing large-scale employee payroll data to (a) appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO), (b) notify the Commissioner of the DPO appointment within 21 days, (c) upon detecting a payroll data breach, notify the Commissioner within 72 hours and affected employees within 7 days. These are materially different obligations from general PDPA compliance â€” the existing module covers data portability and access rights, but not the DPO appointment record-keeping or breach incident management workflows mandated by the February 2025 Commissioner Guidelines.
acceptanceCriteria: Company record stores DPO name, contact email, phone number, appointment date, and Commissioner registration date
System alerts HR Admin if 21-day DPO Commissioner notification deadline not recorded after DPO appointment date

New 'Payroll Data Breach Incident' DocType with fields: discovery_datetime, breach_type, affected_records_count, data_categories_affected, risk_assessment, commissioner_notified_datetime, employee_notification_datetime
72-hour countdown alert activates upon incident creation; red critical alert displayed if commissioner_notified_datetime is blank and 72 hours have elapsed since discovery_datetime
System generates a pre-filled Commissioner Notification Letter (PDF) with breach details, number of affected employees, and risk assessment summary
System generates pre-filled Employee Breach Notification email template once Commissioner has been notified (7-day deadline from Commissioner notification)
Audit log records all breach incident updates with user and timestamp
technicalNotes: New DocType `PDPA Breach Incident` linked to Company; status flow: Draft â†’ Commissioner Notified â†’ Employees Notified â†’ Resolved
DPO fields on Company doctype in `PDPA Compliance` tab via custom field fixture
Commissioner notification required within 72 hours of breach awareness (not breach occurrence) per Guideline on Data Breach Notification (Feb 2025)
Employee notification required within 7 days of Commissioner notification â€” only required if breach poses significant harm to data subjects
Guideline published: https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/en/ â€” Guideline on Data Breach Notification and DPO Appointment

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60F(2): Track 60-Day Hospitalization Leave Quota Separately from Ordinary Sick Leave
userStories.description: The Employment Act 1955 S.60F provides two distinct sick leave entitlements: (1) ordinary paid sick leave of 14-22 days based on service years, and (2) additional hospital/in-patient leave of up to 60 days per year when an employee is hospitalized or requires leave following hospitalization as certified by a registered medical practitioner. These must be tracked separately â€” hospitalization leave does not reduce ordinary sick leave entitlement. The existing 'Track Employment Act S.60F Sick Leave Entitlement per Service Band' story covers ordinary sick leave; this story addresses the distinct 60-day hospitalization leave quota.
acceptanceCriteria: Two distinct Leave Types: 'Sick Leave (EA)' for ordinary sick leave (14-22 days by service band) and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA)' for in-patient/post-hospitalization (60 days annual)
Hospitalization Leave allocation of 60 days auto-created for all EA-covered employees at each leave year start
Hospitalization Leave application requires: `hospitalization_discharge_date` (Date) and `medical_certificate_type` (Select: Hospitalization Certificate | Post-Hospitalization Medical Advice)
Leave balance summary on payslip shows both balances separately: 'Sick Leave (EA): X days' and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA): Y days'
System validates Hospitalization Leave applications are processed against the 60-day quota, not the ordinary sick leave quota
Alert to HR if employee Hospitalization Leave balance reaches zero â€” no further hospitalization leave available for the year
technicalNotes: EA S.60F(2): Up to 60 days paid hospitalization leave when certified by registered medical practitioner that in-patient care is necessary
EA S.60F(1): Ordinary sick leave: 14 days (<2 years service), 18 days (2-5 years), 22 days (>5 years)
Both entitlements paid at ordinary rate of pay
Employee may use both entitlements in same year â€” they are separate pools
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60F â€” https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265).pdf

userStories.title: PCB 2026 Specification: Enforce Truncation-then-5-Cent-Ceiling Rounding Rule in MTD Calculation Engine
userStories.description: The official LHDN PCB 2026 Computerised Calculation Specification mandates a specific two-step rounding rule that is different from standard mathematical rounding: Step 1 â€” truncate the raw PCB amount to exactly 2 decimal places (dropping subsequent digits without rounding); Step 2 â€” apply ceiling rounding up to the nearest 5 cents. For example: 123.023 â†’ truncated to 123.02 â†’ rounded to 123.05; and 123.061 â†’ truncated to 123.06 â†’ rounded to 123.10. If the current MTD engine uses standard banker's rounding or simple 2dp rounding, it will produce incorrect PCB values that diverge from LHDN's expected amounts, potentially causing CP39 submission mismatches.
acceptanceCriteria: MTD calculation engine applies two-step rounding as final step: truncated = floor(raw_pcb * 100) / 100; final_pcb = ceil(truncated / 0.05) * 0.05
All PCB calculation paths (Method 1 regular salary and Method 2 YTD recalculation) pass through the same rounding function
CP39 CSV output values are in 2dp format matching the 5-cent rounded amounts
Dedicated unit test class `TestPCB2026Rounding` covers at least 15 representative scenarios including edge cases at 0.00, 0.01, 0.05, 0.06 fractional values
Rounding function is extracted into a shared utility `pcb_utils.round_pcb(amount)` callable from all calculation paths
technicalNotes: From LHDN PCB 2026 Spec: 'Pengiraan berkomputer PCB hendaklah dalam dua titik perpuluhan dan mesti mengabaikan sebarang angka berikutnya (cth: 123.023 â‰ˆ 123.02). Dari situ, amaun perlu dibundarkan kepada 5 sen yang paling hampir ke atas; cth: 123.02 â‰ˆ 123.05 manakala 123.06 â‰ˆ 123.10'
Use Python Decimal module to avoid floating-point precision errors: from decimal import Decimal, ROUND_DOWN
Step 1: truncated = Decimal(str(raw)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'), rounding=ROUND_DOWN)
Step 2: import math; final = math.ceil(float(truncated) / 0.05) * 0.05; final = Decimal(str(final)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'))
Reference: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf

userStories.title: Enforce e-CP22 Online-Only Mandatory Submission and MyTax Employer Representative Authorization Check
userStories.description: From 1 September 2024, LHDN mandated that all CP22 new employee notifications must be submitted exclusively through the e-CP22 system on MyTax Portal â€” manual paper submissions to LHDN offices are no longer accepted. Critically, the submission can only be made by the company director (with MyTax Employer role) or a registered Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan). The existing 'Auto-Submit CP22 via e-CP22 MyTax Within 30 Days' story focuses on the 30-day deadline tracking; this story enforces the online-only mandate, validates Employer Representative setup, and blocks paper CP22 generation.
acceptanceCriteria: CP22 data generation produces only a digital summary for manual MyTax upload â€” no PDF formatted for paper/postal submission to LHDN office
Company record stores MyTax Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan): name, MyTax login ID, authorization date â€” required field before e-CP22 workflow is accessible
Alert displayed: 'e-CP22 must be submitted by company director or registered Wakil Majikan via mytax.hasil.gov.my â€” system cannot auto-submit'
After manual submission, HR records LHDN acknowledgement reference number on the Employee record (this reference is visible in MyTax after submission)
System blocks new employee onboarding finalization if CP22 status = 'Pending' and 30 days have elapsed since hire date
System shows penalty notice when 30-day deadline at risk: 'Offence under ITA 1967 S.83(2) â€” Fine RM200 to RM20,000 or 6 months imprisonment or both'
technicalNotes: Mandatory online-only e-CP22 submission effective: 1 September 2024
Authorized submitters: Company Director (MyTax Employer role) OR registered Wakil Majikan â€” not HR staff without MyTax authorization
LHDN issues acknowledgement reference upon successful e-CP22 submission â€” must be stored on Employee record for audit
Manual CP22 paper form no longer valid after September 2024 â€” LHDN offices will not accept them
Reference: https://landco.my/social/cp22-online/ and https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/notifications-of-new-employee/

userStories.title: Track EP Category Internship Obligation per 1:3 Internship Policy (TalentCorp/MOHR)
userStories.description: Malaysia's Ministry of Human Resources (MOHR) and TalentCorp mandated that companies employing foreign nationals via Employment Pass (EP) must offer structured paid internships to Malaysian students: 3 placements per EP Cat I, 2 per Cat II, 1 per Cat III. Full enforcement begins 1 January 2026 (pilot extended to March 2026). Payroll must track EP category per expatriate, calculate the total internship quota, and record fulfilled placements (minimum 10 weeks, RM500â€“RM600/month stipend) to produce the annual evidence package supporting future EP renewal applications.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record stores Employment Pass category (Cat I / Cat II / Cat III) with EP approval reference number and approval date
Company dashboard calculates total internship slots required = sum(EP Cat I Ã— 3 + EP Cat II Ã— 2 + EP Cat III Ã— 1), capped at 2% of total employee headcount
Intern Placement doctype records: intern name, qualification level, TalentCorp programme reference, start/end date, stipend amount, fulfilment status
System validates intern stipend meets minimum: RM600/month for degree/master/DLKM level, RM500/month for diploma/SKM/certificate level
Compliance summary report shows total quota vs. fulfilled placements, with gap count highlighted in red when unfulfilled quota > 0
Alert triggered 60 days before EP renewal date if internship quota not fully met for the current 12-month period
technicalNotes: Add `employment_pass_category` (Select: Cat I / Cat II / Cat III) and `ep_approval_reference` fields to Employee fixture
New DocType `Internship Placement` linked to Company with fields: intern_name, qualification_level, talentcorp_reference, start_date, end_date, monthly_stipend, status
Dashboard card on LHDN Payroll workspace showing quota compliance ratio
Pilot phase extended to 31 March 2026; mandatory from 1 January 2026
Non-compliance does not cancel existing EPs but adversely impacts future EP renewal assessment

userStories.title: HRD Corp SBL-KHAS Grant Pre-Approval Compliance and 6-Month Post-Training Claim Deadline Alert
userStories.description: HRD Corp requires employers to submit a training grant application via eTRiS at least 1 working day before training begins â€” applications submitted after training starts are automatically rejected. After training completes, claims must be submitted within 30 days (preferred) and no later than 6 months (hard deadline). The existing 'HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard' story records claims already submitted; this story covers the pre-approval compliance workflow and deadline alert system to prevent rejected or expired claims.
acceptanceCriteria: Training Event doctype has fields: training_start_date, training_end_date, etris_application_date, etris_approval_number, etris_claim_submission_date, grant_amount_approved, grant_amount_claimed
System alerts HR Manager if training_start_date is reached and etris_application_date is blank (pre-approval not submitted)
System flags training as non-HRD-claimable if etris_application_date >= training_start_date (post-training application â€” auto-rejected by HRD Corp)
Alert at 20 days and 28 days after training_end_date if etris_claim_submission_date is blank (30-day deadline approaching)
Critical alert escalated to HR Director at 5 months and 5.5 months after training_end_date if claim still not submitted (6-month absolute deadline)
Dashboard shows: trainings needing pre-approval, expiring claim windows (within 30 days), expired claim windows (failed to claim), and rejected grants with reason codes
technicalNotes: HRD Corp eTRiS portal: hrdcorp.gov.my
Grant approval takes 10-14 working days â€” apply well before training start
SBL-KHAS is the main grant scheme requiring application at least 1 day before training
6-month claim deadline is absolute â€” late submissions cannot be appealed or recovered
This extends the existing levy utilization dashboard by adding pre-training compliance tracking

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End
userStories.description: Under Employment Act 1955 S.60A, unused annual leave that is not taken within the leave year must either be carried forward (by agreement) or paid out at the basic rate. The EA 2022 Amendment strengthened employee protections â€” employers cannot unilaterally forfeit unused leave without the employee's agreement. ERPNext's standard leave module does not enforce Malaysian EA-specific carry-forward rules or the mandatory cash-out offer before forfeiture. Payroll must enforce that expiring leave is either carried forward or paid out, with documented employee consent for any forfeiture.
acceptanceCriteria: Annual Leave (EA) carry-forward is configurable with maximum carry-forward days (default: 1x annual entitlement, e.g., max 22 days)
At leave year-end, system generates 'Leave Expiry Notice' per employee showing: days expiring, cash-out amount at basic daily rate, carry-forward option
HR Manager must record employee's decision (Carry Forward | Cash Out | Mutual Forfeiture Agreement) before closing leave year for that employee
Cash-out of unused annual leave = (Monthly Basic Salary / 26) Ã— leave days, recorded as non-statutory pay component on Salary Slip
System blocks leave year close-off for any employee without a recorded leave expiry decision
Audit log records all leave carry-forward and cash-out decisions with HR manager approval and employee acknowledgement timestamp
technicalNotes: EA S.60A(3): Employer must pay employee for unused annual leave on termination at basic rate
EA S.60A(4): Leave should be utilized within 12 months of entitlement year
Cash-out rate: Basic Rate of Pay per day = Monthly Basic Salary / 26 (or number of working days per month per contract)
EA 2022 Amendment added protections against unilateral forfeiture
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60A

userStories.title: FWCMS Foreign Worker Annual Levy Renewal Expiry Date Tracking and Advance Alert per Employee
userStories.description: Foreign worker levies in Malaysia are paid annually via the FWCMS portal, with the expiry date tied to each individual worker's PLKS/permit period (not a fixed calendar date). The existing 'FWCMS Foreign Worker Levy: 10th-of-Month Payment Deadline Alert' story covers monthly payment monitoring; this story covers the annual renewal cycle per individual worker. Employers with many foreign workers at different renewal dates face compliance risk if individual levy expiry dates are not tracked â€” an expired levy can result in immediate non-compliance and worker removal orders.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record (foreign worker) stores: `fwcms_levy_payment_date`, `fwcms_levy_expiry_date`, `fwcms_receipt_reference`, `fwcms_levy_sector` (Select: Manufacturing | Construction | Plantation | Agriculture | Services)
Levy sector rate table: Manufacturing/Construction/Services = RM1,850/year; Plantation/Agriculture = RM640/year (configurable for future rate changes)
Alerts sent to HR Admin at 90, 60, and 30 days before each worker's individual levy expiry date
Critical alert escalated at 14 days before expiry if renewal receipt not yet recorded
Non-compliant workers dashboard widget: shows workers with levy expiring in <30 days, <60 days, and already expired
Upon recording renewal, system validates receipt reference is provided and auto-calculates next expiry = payment date + 365 days (or per PLKS duration if different)
technicalNotes: FWCMS levy is annual, not monthly â€” renewal date is per worker based on PLKS expiry
Manufacturing, Construction, Services: RM1,850/year per worker
Plantation, Agriculture: RM640/year per worker
Multi-tier levy increase of RM300-500 under deliberation â€” rate table must be configurable
FWCMS portal: https://www.fwcms.gov.my â€” levy paid online, employer receives electronic receipt
Levy paid by employer â€” cannot deduct from employee salary

userStories.title: PDPA 2024: Data Processor Agreement Compliance Checklist for External Payroll Service Vendors
userStories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024, effective June 2025, introduces direct legal obligations on data processors â€” including third-party payroll bureaus, statutory submission agents, and HRMS vendors who process employee salary, PCB, EPF, and statutory data. Employers using external payroll services must ensure a valid Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is in place with each processor. This is distinct from the existing DPO appointment and breach notification stories â€” it specifically addresses the contractual compliance layer between the employer (data controller) and any external party handling payroll data.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Data Processor Registry' section on Company record listing all third-party data processors with: processor_name, services_provided, dpa_signed_date, dpa_expiry_date, dpa_document_attachment, last_security_audit_date
Alert to HR/Legal when any DPA expires within 30 days or has expired
Export of employee personal data (salary, IC number, TIN, bank account) triggers an audit log entry showing: user, data category, downstream processor, timestamp
Compliance checklist report confirms: DPA on file (Yes/No), DPA current (Yes/No), DPO notified of processor (Yes/No) per each registered processor
System warns if bulk payroll data export is attempted without any active DPA on file for the receiving processor
technicalNotes: PDPA 2024 S.XX: Data processors now have direct liability for security principle breaches â€” fine up to RM1,000,000 or 3 years imprisonment
DPA must specify: purpose of processing, data categories, retention period, security measures, data subject rights handling
Effective date of data processor direct obligations: 1 June 2025
Processors typically include: HRD Corp submission agents, LHDN e-PCB Plus submission bureaus, bank payroll disbursement services, EPF/SOCSO e-filing vendors
Reference: https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Act-A1727.pdf

userStories.title: TP1(1/2026): Capture New Domestic Tourism Attraction Expenses Relief (RM1,000) in Employee PCB Declaration
userStories.description: Malaysia Budget 2026 introduced a brand-new individual income tax relief category in the TP1(1/2026) form: expenses for entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes conducted within Malaysia, capped at RM1,000 per year of assessment. This category has never appeared in any prior TP1 form. The PCB engine must add this as a new deductible item when employees declare it on their TP1(1/2026) relief form. While the generic 'Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories' story addresses the budget's general TP1 changes, domestic tourism is an entirely new relief code with no prior-year equivalent requiring discrete field mapping, validation, and PCB computation integration.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration form adds field: `domestic_tourism_expenses` (Currency, label: 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions / art & cultural programmes in Malaysia')
PCB calculation deducts declared `domestic_tourism_expenses` (capped at RM1,000) from chargeable income when computing monthly MTD
Field only available in TP1(1/2026) form â€” not retroactively applied to prior-year TP1 records
Validation: amount cannot exceed RM1,000; system shows 'Maximum RM1,000 for YA2026 domestic tourism relief' if exceeded
Year-end PCB reconciliation report shows domestic tourism relief declared per employee
This field is absent from TP3(1/2026) â€” domestic tourism is a current-employer TP1 item, not a prior-employer YTD income item
technicalNotes: From LHDN TP1(1/2026) explanatory notes: new relief for 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes within Malaysia'
Maximum: RM1,000 per year of assessment (YA2026 only â€” no retroactive application)
Source form: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/gjuhcbc2/bi-tp1-form-2026.pdf
Explanatory notes: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/i1lcd0wn/bi-explanatory-notes-for-from-tp1-2026.pdf
Confirmed also by briohr.com YA2026 update: 'New Reliefs: Domestic Tourism Expenses â€” Entrance fees to local tourist attractions â€” Relief cap: RM1,000'

userStories.title: MySIP Structured Internship Stipend Minimum Threshold Validation and Double Tax Deduction Payroll Tagging
userStories.description: Employers participating in TalentCorp's National Structured Internship Programme (MySIP) are entitled to a double corporate tax deduction on internship-related expenses (stipends, training costs) from YA 2017 to YA 2025, with potential extension. Payroll must identify intern employees, validate their monthly stipend meets the MySIP minimum threshold, track the TalentCorp Endorsement Letter reference, and produce a year-end summary of MySIP-qualifying expenses per intern for the corporate tax claim. This is a corporate tax benefit tracking feature, not a statutory payroll deduction.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record has flag `is_mysip_intern` (Check) and fields: `mysip_qualification_level` (Select: Master/Degree/DLKM | Diploma/SKM/Certificate), `talentcorp_endorsement_reference` (Data)
On payroll run, if `is_mysip_intern` = true, system validates stipend >= RM600 for degree-level and >= RM500 for diploma-level interns; warning displayed if below threshold
Year-end MySIP Summary Report shows: intern name, qualification level, endorsement reference, total stipend paid, total qualifying expenses for double deduction claim
Report includes total claimable double deduction amount = 2x (total stipend + training costs)
Each qualifying Salary Slip is tagged with `mysip_eligible: true` for audit trail
technicalNotes: Minimum stipends: RM600/month for Master, Degree, DLKM, Professional Certificate; RM500/month for Diploma, DKM, SKM Level 1-3
Double deduction applies to: intern allowances, training materials, accommodation/transport
TalentCorp issues Endorsement Letter based on annual internship placements submitted
MySIP double deduction covered YA2017-YA2025; check Budget 2026 for extension status
Reference: https://www.talentcorp.com.my/mysip-incentives/

userStories.title: Budget 2026 Expanded Double Deduction for Training Disabled Care Workers (YA2026-2027) â€” Payroll Evidence Package
userStories.description: Malaysia Budget 2026 expanded the existing double tax deduction for sponsoring OKU training to also cover employers who sponsor training costs for care workers who support OKU (Orang Kurang Upaya) individuals, effective YA2026 and YA2027 only. This is a new category distinct from the existing OKU employee double deduction and the general HRD Corp levy claims. Payroll must distinguish care worker training from regular training, apply the YA2026-2027 date gate, and generate the annual evidence schedule for corporate tax submission.
acceptanceCriteria: Training Event doctype has `deduction_category` field (Select: Standard | HRD Corp | OKU Employee Training | OKU Care Worker Training [Budget 2026])
When `deduction_category` = 'OKU Care Worker Training', system flags the record as qualifying for Budget 2026 double deduction
Date gate: OKU Care Worker Training double deduction only valid for training completed between 1 January 2026 and 31 December 2027; system blocks this category for training outside this range
Year-end Summary Report shows separate line: 'Budget 2026 OKU Care Worker Training Double Deduction â€” Qualifying Expenses: RM X, Claimable Deduction: RM 2X'
Alert if 'OKU Care Worker Training' is selected for training outside YA2026-2027 range
technicalNotes: From Budget 2026: 'The double deduction for sponsoring training for persons with disabilities is expanded to include sponsoring training for care workers for YA2026-YA2027'
Care worker = workers providing direct care/support services to OKU individuals (not the OKU employee themselves)
Qualifying expenses: course fees, training materials, trainer fees â€” same rules as standard OKU training deduction
This is a corporate income tax deduction claimed in the company's annual return â€” not a payroll withholding deduction

Source: Crowe Malaysia Budget 2026 Highlights â€” https://www.crowe.com/my/-/media/crowe/firms/asia-pacific/my/crowemy/news/key-highlights-of-malaysias-budget-2026--for-individuals-and-businesses-newsletter.pdf

userStories.title: PCB Under-Deduction Employer Liability Alert (Section 107A ITA 1967)
userStories.description: Under Section 107A of the Income Tax Act 1967, employers are personally liable for any shortfall in monthly PCB deductions â€” if an employee's PCB is under-deducted, LHDN can assess the employer for the full uncollected tax plus penalties. The payroll system must detect anomalous PCB under-deduction patterns before Salary Slips are finalised and require mandatory payroll admin acknowledgement with documented justification before the slip can proceed.
acceptanceCriteria: System flags a Salary Slip where PCB drops more than 50% vs. the prior month without a corresponding documented change in income or declared relief values
Alert raised when an employee's computed PCB is zero but their chargeable income (after EPF and declared reliefs) exceeds the applicable zero-tax threshold
Alert is displayed as a mandatory acknowledgement dialog that must be completed before the Salary Slip can be submitted
Payroll admin acknowledgement reason is captured and stored in the PCB Change Audit Trail DocType with timestamp and user
Monthly compliance report summarises all flagged and acknowledged under-deduction cases for LHDN audit readiness
technicalNotes: Section 107A ITA 1967: employer personally liable for uncollected PCB if employee later cannot pay
Zero-tax threshold for single monthly-salaried employee: chargeable income â‰¤ RM2,851/month after EPF and standard RM9,000 individual relief annualised and monthly-ised
Threshold: flag if PCB drops >50% month-over-month without matching income/relief change event
Complements existing 'Implement PCB Change Audit Trail DocType' story
LHDN MTD Payment Guidelines: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/mtd-payment/
dependencies: Implement PCB Change Audit Trail DocType
Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1: Permanent RM3,000 Childcare Relief for JKM-Registered Transit Centres
userStories.description: Budget 2026 merged the time-bound RM1,000 childcare relief with the existing RM2,000 relief into a permanent RM3,000 combined relief, and expanded eligible premises to include daily care and after-school transit centres registered with the Department of Social Welfare (JKM) for children up to 12 years. The TP1(1/2026) form and PCB computation engine must reflect this permanent change effective YA2026.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1(1/2026) childcare relief field accepts up to RM3,000 (removing the old RM2,000 regular + RM1,000 time-bound split structure)
System does not apply any year-based expiry restriction to the combined RM3,000 amount for YA2026 onward
TP1 help text and eligibility description updated to include JKM-registered after-school transit centres for children aged â‰¤12
PCB calculation applies the correct RM3,000 ceiling for this relief line item in the MTD engine
Historical TP1 records for YA2025 and prior retain the old RM2,000 ceiling (no retrospective change)
technicalNotes: Update TP1 DocType relief item field: `childcare_relief_amount` max validation from 2000 to 3000
Remove time-bound relief year guard that previously expired the RM1,000 component
Effective: YA2026 per Finance Bill 2025, gazetted December 2025
Reference: LHDN TP1(1/2026) form, PCB 2026 Specification Amendment
dependencies: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1: Increase Children's Learning Disability Diagnostic Relief to RM10,000
userStories.description: Budget 2026 increased the individual income tax relief for diagnostic, early intervention, and rehabilitation treatment expenses for children under 18 with learning disabilities (autism, ADHD, global developmental delay, intellectual disability, Down syndrome, specific learning disabilities) from RM6,000 to RM10,000. The TP1 form ceiling and PCB engine must be updated for YA2026 onward.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1(1/2026) learning disability relief field maximum updated from RM6,000 to RM10,000
PCB computation engine caps this relief at RM10,000 for assessment years >= 2026
EA Form Section A correctly reflects the RM10,000 maximum when generating for YA2026
Historical TP1 records for YA2025 and prior retain the RM6,000 cap with no retrospective change
Validation error message updated: 'Maximum RM10,000 for YA2026 children learning disability relief'
technicalNotes: Update TP1 DocType: `learning_disability_relief_amount` max validation from 6000 to 10000
Add assessment year guard: RM10,000 cap only applies for assessment_year >= 2026
Budget 2026 / Finance Bill 2025, effective YA2026
Eligible conditions: autism, ADHD, global developmental delay (GDD), intellectual disability, Down syndrome, specific learning disabilities (dyslexia, dyspraxia, dyscalculia)
dependencies: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes

userStories.title: Process TP3(1/2026) Updated Form Format with Budget 2026 Relief Line Items
userStories.description: LHDN released the updated TP3(1/2026) form as part of the PCB 2026 specification amendments to accommodate Budget 2026 relief changes. When new employees join mid-year and declare income from a prior employer, the TP3 declaration must use the 2026 form structure which includes updated YA2026 relief line items (e.g., childcare RM3,000, learning disability RM10,000, expanded vaccination, eco-friendly lifestyle items). The payroll system must ingest and process the TP3(1/2026) format correctly so that prior-employer relief values flow into the new employer's PCB computation.
acceptanceCriteria: New hire TP3 entry UI displays all TP3(1/2026) form fields including the Budget 2026 relief items from the prior employer
System selects TP3 form version based on employee join year: joiners in 2026 use TP3(1/2026); prior joiners use the applicable year form
Prior-employer relief values from TP3(1/2026) (gross income, EPF/SOCSO/EIS contributions, PCB paid, Zakat, all YA2026 reliefs) flow into the new employer's MTD Method 1 or Method 2 calculation
TP3 record stores form version (`tp3_form_version` = '1/2026') alongside the declaration data
PCB computed for the first month at new employer correctly incorporates TP3(1/2026) carry-forward amounts
technicalNotes: Form: PCB TP3 (1/2026) â€” Previous Employer Employment Information in Current Year For MTD Purpose
LHDN PCB 2026 specification amendment includes TP3(1/2026) format update alongside TP1(1/2026)
Carry-forward values feed into MTD computation engine: prior_gross_income, prior_epf, prior_pcb_paid, prior_reliefs_declared
Official spec: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/employer-payroll-data-specification/
dependencies: Implement TP3 Carry-Forward Declaration for New Hires (Prior Employer YTD)

userStories.title: TP3 30-Day Collection Deadline Compliance Alert for Mid-Year New Hires
userStories.description: LHDN regulations require employees with prior-year income to submit the TP3 form to their new employer within 30 days of joining, and the employer must process it before computing the first monthly PCB. Without TP3, PCB is computed as if the employee has no prior income, systematically under-deducting tax and creating employer audit liability. The system must automatically alert HR when a mid-year new hire is approaching or has exceeded the 30-day TP3 collection deadline.
acceptanceCriteria: System generates a task and email notification 14 days after a new hire's join date when no TP3 record exists and join date is after 1 January of the current year
Red warning banner displayed on first Salary Slip generation for any employee joined after January 1 who has no TP3 record on file
HR dashboard shows a list of employees with outstanding TP3 submissions beyond 30 days from their join date
Alert is automatically cleared once a TP3 record is saved and linked to the employee
January joiners (join date = 1 January to 31 January, first full month) are excluded from the alert â€” they have no prior-employer income in the current year
technicalNotes: Scheduled daily background job: iterate Employee records where date_of_joining > current year Jan 1 AND no TP3 DocType entry linked
Notification sent via Frappe notification system to HR Manager role
LHDN TP3 guidance: employee should submit TP3 within 30 days of joining; employer should process before first payroll
Penalty for employer non-compliance with incorrect PCB: Section 107A ITA 1967 employer liability
dependencies: Implement TP3 Carry-Forward Declaration for New Hires (Prior Employer YTD)

userStories.title: Worker Classification Flag: Contract-of-Service vs Contract-for-Service for PCB/WHT Routing
userStories.description: Employees under a Contract of Service (employment contract) are subject to monthly PCB deduction under Section 107A ITA 1967. Workers engaged under a Contract for Service (independent contractors, freelancers, agents) are instead subject to Withholding Tax under Section 107D ITA 1967 and CP58 reporting â€” not PCB. Misclassification leads to incorrect statutory deductions, potential penalties, and LHDN audit findings. The system needs an explicit classification flag at the worker record level that automatically routes deductions to the correct statutory scheme.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType has `custom_contract_type` field (Select: 'Contract of Service' [default] | 'Contract for Service')
PCB/MTD deductions on Salary Slip apply only for 'Contract of Service' workers
System raises a warning when a 'Contract for Service' worker is added to a Salary Slip payroll run (incorrect workflow â€” contractor payments use Supplier/Purchase Invoice)
WHT deduction (Section 107D, 2%) and CP58 reporting trigger for 'Contract for Service' workers
Classification changes are logged in an audit trail with timestamp, changed-by user, and reason
technicalNotes: LHDN Public Ruling 3/2019: guidelines to distinguish employment (contract of service) from self-employment (contract for service)
PCB applies under Section 107A ITA 1967 (employer withholding for employees)
WHT applies under Section 107D ITA 1967 (payer deduction for agents/dealers/distributors)
Complements and gates the existing 'Section 107D ITA â€” WHT 2% Deduction, Monthly Remittance, and CP58 Integration' story
dependencies: Section 107D ITA â€” WHT 2% Deduction, Monthly Remittance, and CP58 Integration for Agent/Dealer Payments

userStories.title: PCB Year-to-Date Arrears Recovery from Final Pay on Employee Termination
userStories.description: When an employee terminates mid-year, any accumulated PCB shortfall (from under-deduction in prior months due to relief changes or calculation errors) must be recovered from the final salary payment as LHDN holds the employer liable for the full year-to-date MTD. The system should automatically compute the YTD PCB liability versus YTD PCB actually deducted, and add any shortfall as a 'PCB Arrears Recovery' deduction on the final Salary Slip before the employee exits.
acceptanceCriteria: On generating the final-month Salary Slip for a terminating employee, system computes: (total MTD required YTD) minus (total PCB deducted YTD from all prior submitted Salary Slips)
Positive shortfall (under-deduction) added as a 'PCB Arrears Recovery' deduction line on the final Salary Slip with clear labelling
If the shortfall exceeds the available final net pay, system alerts HR with the outstanding unrecoverable amount and links to the CP21/CP22A workflow for LHDN notification
Zero or negative difference (over-deduction) displayed as a refund advisory note for the employee's self-filing
Final EA Form total PCB includes the arrears recovery amount deducted in the final month
technicalNotes: Query all submitted Salary Slips for the employee in the current assessment year to sum `custom_pcb_amount` deducted
Recompute total annual MTD required using MTD Method 2 year-to-date formula at the annualised rate for the months worked
Difference = Required YTD MTD âˆ’ Actual YTD MTD deducted; positive value = shortfall to recover
Links to: CP21 workflow (departure clearance), CP22A (retirement/resignation notification)
LHDN: Termination of Service / Employment obligations â€” https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/individual/others/termination-of-service-employment/
dependencies: Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)
Add CP21 workflow for employee cessation and departure from Malaysia

userStories.title: HRD Corp eTRIS Monthly Levy Payment Deadline Alert and Payment Reference Tracking
userStories.description: HRD Corp requires employers to pay the monthly Human Resource Development levy via the eTRIS portal by the 15th of the following month, with late payment incurring a 10% penalty plus RM2/day. While existing stories cover levy calculation and payment file generation, there is no mechanism to track whether the actual eTRIS payment has been made, record the FPX/JomPay payment reference number, and alert payroll admin when the 15th deadline is approaching with an unpaid balance.
acceptanceCriteria: System generates an automated reminder task/notification to HR Admin on the 10th of each month for the prior month's outstanding HRD Corp levy payment
Payroll admin can record eTRIS payment confirmation: payment_date, payment_method (FPX | JomPay), payment_reference_number per monthly period
Compliance dashboard flags any levy period where payment has not been recorded after the 15th of the month as 'Overdue'
Monthly levy payment summary report shows: period, levy amount, payment status (Paid / Unpaid / Overdue), payment date, eTRIS reference number
Escalation notification sent to HR Manager and Finance Manager when levy remains unpaid after the 20th of the month (5-day late buffer)
technicalNotes: Create 'HRD Corp Levy Payment' DocType: period_month, levy_amount, payment_date, payment_method, payment_reference, status (Unpaid/Paid/Overdue)
Scheduled daily job: set status = 'Overdue' where payment_date is null and today > 15th of the following month
Distinct from: 'HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard' (training claim tracking) and 'HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File' (file generation)
HRD Corp late penalty: 10% of outstanding levy + RM2/day; cumulative until paid
eTRIS portal: portal.etris.hrdcorp.gov.my; accepted payment methods: FPX, JomPay
dependencies: HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File and HRDF Employer Portal Integration
Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1: Expand Vaccination Relief Scope to All NPRA-Approved Vaccines
userStories.description: Budget 2026 expanded the RM1,000 vaccination relief (claimable for self, spouse, and children) from a restricted list of named vaccines to all vaccines registered and approved by the National Pharmaceutical Regulatory Agency (NPRA), Ministry of Health. The TP1 form eligibility description must be updated to remove the previously named vaccine restriction so employees can claim any NPRA-registered vaccine expense.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1(1/2026) vaccination relief field description updated to read 'All vaccines registered and approved by NPRA, Ministry of Health' (removing specific vaccine names)
System applies RM1,000 ceiling based on amount only; no vaccine-type restriction or NPRA lookup required
PCB computation includes vaccination relief up to RM1,000 per assessment year for eligible employees
TP1 audit trail and EA Form reflect the updated description for YA2026 declarations
Historical TP1 records for YA2025 retain old description (no retroactive change to prior-year records)
technicalNotes: Update is description/help text only â€” the relief ceiling of RM1,000 is unchanged
No external NPRA API lookup required; amount-based validation only
Effective YA2026 per Finance Bill 2025
NPRA: National Pharmaceutical Regulatory Agency, Ministry of Health Malaysia
dependencies: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1: Add Eco-Friendly Lifestyle Items to RM2,500 Lifestyle Relief (YA2026-YA2027)
userStories.description: Budget 2026 added food waste disposers and home CCTV systems to the existing RM2,500 lifestyle tax relief, claimable once across YA2026 and YA2027 combined. The TP1 form must capture this new eco-friendly subcategory with a two-year one-time claim flag, sharing the overall RM2,500 lifestyle relief ceiling with other qualifying items.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1(1/2026) lifestyle relief section includes an 'Eco-Friendly Lifestyle Items' sub-category (food waste disposers, home CCTV systems)
System enforces one-time claim restriction: if claimed in YA2026, the sub-category is blocked for YA2027 for the same employee
Eco-friendly items share the combined RM2,500 lifestyle ceiling; combined total cannot exceed RM2,500
PCB computation incorporates eco-friendly items within the overall RM2,500 lifestyle ceiling
Assessment year claim flag stored per employee: `custom_eco_lifestyle_claimed_ya` to enforce the two-year window
technicalNotes: Add `custom_eco_lifestyle_claimed_ya` (Int) field on Employee DocType to track the claim year
Block eco-friendly sub-category if `custom_eco_lifestyle_claimed_ya` is already set for a prior year within YA2026-YA2027
Overall lifestyle relief ceiling remains RM2,500; eco-friendly subcategory shares this pool
Finance Bill 2025 / Budget 2026, effective YA2026-YA2027 only â€” auto-disable after YA2027
dependencies: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes

userStories.title: Annual Leave Cash-Out PCB Computation and EA Form Income Classification
userStories.description: When employees cash out accumulated annual leave under Employment Act S.60A, the cash-out payment is additional employment income in the month of disbursement and is fully subject to PCB. The system must aggregate the cash-out amount with the employee's regular monthly salary for PCB computation purposes, and classify the payment correctly on the EA Form as gross employment income, not as a separate allowance or exempted item.
acceptanceCriteria: Annual leave cash-out Salary Component is flagged as PCB-taxable income (not exempt)
PCB for the payment month is computed on the aggregate of regular gross salary plus the leave cash-out amount
Cash-out amount appears on the EA Form under gross employment income (basic salary/wages line in Section A), not in other income categories
Salary Slip labels the cash-out line item clearly and separately from regular salary for employee transparency
Year-end EA Form reconciliation correctly includes the cash-out amount in total employment income per employee
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.60A(3): employer must pay employee for unused leave at basic rate of pay
Cash-out treated as additional salary in the payment month for PCB purposes (not the irregular/bonus PCB formula unless employer specifically elects that method)
Cash-out rate: Monthly Basic Salary Ã· 26 Ã— number of leave days cashed out
Link to 'Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End' story for leave quantum determination
dependencies: Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End
Add bonus/irregular payment classification for PCB differentiation

userStories.title: Gross-Up PCB Calculation for Net Pay Guarantee Employment Agreements
userStories.description: Some employment contracts â€” especially for expatriates and senior executives â€” guarantee the employee a specific net pay, with the employer bearing all PCB costs. In these cases, the employer-borne PCB is itself a taxable benefit-in-kind, creating a circular gross-up calculation that requires an iterative formula. The system must implement the gross-up computation to derive the correct gross salary and PCB amount, and reflect the employer-borne PCB as a BIK on the EA Form.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record has a boolean field `custom_net_pay_guarantee` indicating the employer bears PCB on the employee's behalf
When enabled, Salary Slip applies iterative gross-up: compute gross such that PCB(gross) + net = gross (converge within 5 iterations, accuracy RM0.10)
Employer-borne PCB amount is shown as a BIK line on the EA Form Section C
Salary Slip notes section shows the gross-up computation breakdown: guaranteed net, computed gross, PCB on gross
PCB is computed on the grossed-up gross salary using the standard MTD formula
Gross-up applies only to base salary; EPF and other deductions computed before gross-up
technicalNotes: Gross-up formula (iterative): Gross_n+1 = Net + PCB(Gross_n); converge when |Gross_n+1 - Gross_n| < 0.10
Use Python Decimal module to avoid floating-point errors in convergence loop
Employer-borne PCB is a BIK per LHDN guidelines â€” must be included on EA Form as taxable benefit
Common in C-suite contracts and expatriate relocation packages
Complements 'Implement Expatriate Gross-Up Calculator and DTA Country Table' (which focuses on DTA/non-resident; this covers net pay guarantee for any employee)
dependencies: Implement PCB/MTD monthly calculation validation
Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

userStories.title: Store Employee TIN on Employee DocType and Validate for e-PCB Plus Upload
userStories.description: LHDN's e-PCB Plus system (mandatory from 2025) requires each employee's Tax Identification Number (TIN) to be included in the e-Data PCB upload file. LHDN explicitly instructs employers to update employee TIN details in their payroll system prior to submission â€” incomplete or missing TIN data causes the upload to be rejected. Currently the app stores the employee's NRIC/Passport but may not capture TIN as a separate mandatory field validated before CP39 export.
acceptanceCriteria: A `custom_employee_tin` field exists on the Employee DocType and is included in the custom_field.json fixture
The CP39 / e-Data PCB export includes the employee TIN column in the correct position per LHDN e-PCB Plus file specification
Payroll processing raises a validation warning (not hard block) if any employee in the pay period has a blank TIN
A pre-submission validation report lists all employees with missing TIN, so HR can bulk-update before CP39 export
TIN format is validated: Malaysian TIN is either IG/SG/OG/D/C prefix followed by 11 digits
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus upload file (e-Data PCB .txt format) requires employee TIN in field position per LHDN e-PCB Plus specification
LHDN notice: 'Employers using e-Data PCB are requested to update the TIN details of their employees in their payroll system'
Reference: LHDN e-PCB Plus Transition (https://pandahrms.com/lhdns-early-announcement-transition-to-e-pcb-plus-system/)
Malaysian TIN formats: IG (individual), SG (salary earner), OG (business), D (company), C (company) â€” payroll employees will be IG or SG prefix
dependencies: Fix CP39 CSV Format â€” Add All Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns

userStories.title: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025
userStories.description: The existing minimum wage story covers the RM1,700 requirement effective February 2025 for MASCO employers and those with 5+ employees. However, from 1 August 2025, the RM1,700 minimum wage universally extends to ALL employers regardless of headcount â€” including employers with fewer than 5 employees who were previously on the RM1,500 rate. Non-compliance carries fines up to RM10,000 per affected employee per offence. The payroll system must update its minimum wage validation logic to apply RM1,700 universally from August 2025 payroll, removing any employee-count conditional.
acceptanceCriteria: Minimum wage validation in payroll uses RM1,700 as the floor for ALL employees regardless of employer headcount, effective for payroll periods from August 2025 onward
The existing employee-count threshold conditional (5+ employees for RM1,700 vs <5 for RM1,500) is removed from the validation engine for payroll periods >= 2025-08-01
A one-time migration alert notifies HR of any employees currently paid between RM1,500 and RM1,699.99 who will be in violation from August 2025
Payslip submission for August 2025 or later with basic salary below RM1,700 raises a hard validation error citing 'Minimum Wages Order 2024 â€” universal enforcement from 1 August 2025'
The penalty warning text is updated to reflect RM10,000 per employee fine for non-compliance
technicalNotes: Minimum Wages Order 2024: RM1,700 universal effective 1 August 2025 per MOHR gazette
Previously: RM1,700 applied to MASCO employers from Feb 2025 and employers with 5+ from Feb 2025; <5 non-MASCO employers had until Aug 2025 grace
Foreign workers are covered; domestic workers remain exempt
Reference: MOHR announcement (https://pandahrms.com/rm1700-minimum-wage-applies-to-all-employers-starting-1-august-2025/)
dependencies: Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700 (Feb 2025 Amendment)

userStories.title: Implement TP2 Form â€” Employee Irrevocable BIK/VOLA Election for PCB Inclusion
userStories.description: LHDN allows employees to make an irrevocable election within a tax year to include Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) and the Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA) as part of their monthly remuneration for MTD/PCB purposes by submitting Form TP2 to the employer. Without TP2 handling, BIK and VOLA are excluded from PCB by default, meaning employees who prefer MTD spreading over lump-sum year-end payment have no mechanism to elect this. The employer must honour the election, adjust PCB computation for the remainder of the year, and retain the TP2 for 7 years.
acceptanceCriteria: A TP2 Election child table or linked DocType on the Employee record captures: election date, BIK monthly value, VOLA monthly value, employer approval status, and effective month
Once approved by employer, the BIK + VOLA amounts are added to the employee's gross remuneration base used in PCB calculation for all subsequent payroll runs within the same assessment year
The election is flagged irrevocable â€” system prevents reversal within the same assessment year under the same employer
BIK/VOLA amounts added via TP2 do NOT appear in gross salary on the payslip or EA Form gross salary field; they only affect the PCB engine input
System generates a TP2 record PDF with employee name, IC number, employer name, election amounts, and approval signature block
TP2 records are flagged with a 7-year retention requirement in the payroll audit log
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB Specification 2025: BIK/VOLA elected via TP2 is added to gross remuneration for MTD calculation only (not for payslip/EA gross salary)
TP2 is not submitted to LHDN â€” it is an internal employer record required to be retained 7 years under Section 82 ITA
Reference: LHDN Specification for Computerised PCB Calculation 2025 (https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf)
The irrevocability clause means the PCB engine must lock the BIK/VOLA addend for assessment year once set
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

userStories.title: Enforce ITA Section 82 7-Year Payroll Tax Record Retention (Separate from MOHR 6-Year)
userStories.description: Section 82 of the Income Tax Act 1967 requires employers to keep all payroll-related tax records (EA forms, CP39 files, Borang E acknowledgements, TP1/TP2/TP3 forms, MTD payment receipts) for 7 years from the end of the assessment year. This is a separate and longer obligation than the Employment Act's 6-year payroll register requirement (already covered). Non-compliance means LHDN can assess tax on a best-judgment basis and impose penalties. The system must enforce a 7-year retention lock on LHDN-related payroll records distinct from the MOHR 6-year lock.
acceptanceCriteria: A `lhdn_retention_expiry_date` field is set on LHDN-related records (EA Form, CP39 run, Borang E, TP1/TP2/TP3) as assessment_year + 7 years
Attempting to delete or archive LHDN records before their `lhdn_retention_expiry_date` raises a blocking validation error citing 'ITA Section 82 â€” 7-year retention'
A scheduled report runs annually listing LHDN records eligible for deletion (past their 7-year expiry) for HR Manager review
The retention lock applies independently of the Employment Act 6-year lock on payslips â€” an EA Form for YA2019 (issued Feb 2020) cannot be deleted before Feb 2027
System clearly labels the retention reason as 'ITA Section 82' vs 'Employment Act S.60A' so audit trails distinguish the two obligations
technicalNotes: ITA Section 82: records must be kept 7 years from the end of the year to which any income relates
LHDN Public Ruling No. 5/2000 (revised) defines what constitutes 'sufficient records' including payroll records, EA forms, and MTD receipts
Reference: ANC Group explanation of Section 82 (https://ancgroup.biz/keep-sufficient-records-for-7-years/)
Existing story 'Implement Payroll Wage Register with 6-Year Retention' covers MOHR Employment Act obligation â€” this story is additive for LHDN-specific records
dependencies: Implement Payroll Wage Register with 6-Year Retention and LHDN/MOHR Audit Export

userStories.title: Generate LHDN PCB 2026 Specification Annual Update Checklist and Version Flag
userStories.description: LHDN publishes an updated 'Specification for Computerised PCB Calculation' (Spesifikasi Kaedah Pengiraan Berkomputer PCB) each year. For 2025 and 2026, separate specification PDFs exist with different TP1 deduction items, relief ceilings, and MTD formula amendments. The app currently has no mechanism to track which PCB specification version is active, making it difficult for HR administrators to verify that the calculation engine is aligned with the current year's LHDN-published specification. A version flag and annual update checklist is needed.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'PCB Specification Version' setting in LHDN Payroll Settings stores the active specification year (e.g., '2025' or '2026') with a link to the LHDN PDF URL
Switching the specification version triggers a checklist of changed parameters between versions that an HR administrator must review and confirm
The PCB calculation engine reads the active specification year to select the correct TP1 relief ceilings, Schedule 1 tax brackets, and rounding rules for that year
On each payroll run, the system logs which PCB specification version was used â€” visible in the payroll audit trail
A warning is shown in January each year prompting HR to verify whether LHDN has published an updated specification for the new assessment year
technicalNotes: LHDN 2025 PCB Specification: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
LHDN 2026 PCB Specification: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf
The 2026 specification confirmed no change to the MTD formula but amended TP1 deduction items
Version management prevents silent errors when LHDN changes reliefs between assessment years
dependencies: Apply YA2025 PCB Schedule 1 Tax Brackets Including New 30% Tier for Income Exceeding RM2 Million

userStories.title: Implement Employment Act S.25A Digital Payslip Mandatory Fields Compliance Validator
userStories.description: Employment Act Section 25A (as amended by Employment (Amendment) Act 2022) requires employers to provide a payslip to every employee at the time of each wage payment. The payslip must contain mandatory fields including: employee full name, IC/passport number, gender, citizenship status, wage period, wage amount, payment date, overtime earnings, deductions itemised, and employer name. The existing payslip generation may not validate all mandatory fields before generation, exposing employers to MOHR inspection penalties. This story adds a mandatory-field compliance validator to payslip generation.
acceptanceCriteria: Before a Salary Slip is submitted, a validator checks that the generated payslip PDF/print includes: employee full name, IC/passport number, wage period (start and end date), gross salary, itemised deductions (EPF, SOCSO, EIS, PCB, each as separate lines), net pay, and payment date
Missing any mandatory field raises a hard validation error citing 'Employment Act S.25A â€” Payslip mandatory fields'
The payslip print template is updated to include gender and citizenship status fields if currently absent
A payslip compliance audit report lists any historical salary slips missing mandatory fields
The system confirms LHDN statutory deductions (PCB, EPF, SOCSO, EIS) are itemised individually on the payslip â€” not combined into a single 'deductions' total
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.25A (post-2022 amendment): employer must give payslip at every wage payment
MOHR inspection can impose fines for non-compliant payslips
Mandatory fields per EA S.25A: name, employee ID, gender, citizenship, wage period, amount, date, all payments/deductions with particulars
Reference: Employment Act 1955 (Akta 265) Section 25A (https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265)_0.pdf)
dependencies: Generate Employment Act S.61 Compliant Payslip with All Mandatory Fields

userStories.title: CP500 Bi-Monthly Advance Tax Installment Awareness Panel for Directors with Non-Salary Income
userStories.description: Directors and sole proprietors who have non-employment income (dividends, rental, business profits) receive LHDN CP500 notices requiring 6 bi-monthly advance tax installments in March, May, July, September, November, and January. ERPNext payroll operates the PCB deduction for salary, but does not track whether the same director also has CP500 obligations outside payroll. HR/payroll admins need visibility into which directors are CP500 payers so they can advise against over-deducting PCB on salary and help directors avoid double-paying or underpaying. LHDN intensified CP500 issuance in 2025-2026.
acceptanceCriteria: A `cp500_payer` boolean flag and `cp500_annual_installment_amount` field can be set on the Director/Employee record
If `cp500_payer = True`, the payroll summary for that director includes an advisory note: 'Employee has CP500 obligations â€” confirm PCB deduction does not exceed actual tax liability'
A CP500 Installment Tracker child table on the Employee allows recording installment due dates (Mar/May/Jul/Sep/Nov/Jan) and payment reference numbers
Dashboard widget on LHDN Payroll Workspace shows directors with upcoming CP500 installment due dates within next 30 days
System does not auto-deduct CP500 (it is paid directly by the director to LHDN) â€” it is advisory and tracking only
technicalNotes: CP500 is issued by LHDN to individuals with non-salary income; 6 bi-monthly installments across the calendar year
The payment schedule: installment months are March, May, July, September, November, January
Revision of CP500 amount: first revision by 30 June, second by 31 October of the assessment year
Reference: LHDN CP500 guide (https://www.ajobthing.com/resources/blog/cp500-installment-pay-tax-malaysia)

userStories.title: e-Lejar Employer Tax Account Ledger Reconciliation Link and PCB Payment Verification
userStories.description: LHDN's e-Lejar application (accessible via MyTax portal) allows employers to check their employer tax account ledger transactions â€” including PCB remittances received, assessments raised, and refunds â€” at any time. Currently the lhdn_payroll_integration app tracks submission references but has no way to cross-check whether LHDN's ledger matches the amounts the employer remitted. Employers frequently discover discrepancies (e.g., payments credited to wrong month, rejected submissions not re-submitted) only during year-end Borang E reconciliation. A link to e-Lejar and a monthly reconciliation workflow would close this gap.
acceptanceCriteria: LHDN Developer Tools page includes a direct link to e-Lejar (https://mytax.hasil.gov.my) with instructions for employer ledger access
A 'PCB Ledger Reconciliation' panel allows HR to input LHDN's e-Lejar monthly credited amounts and compare against the sum of CP39 submissions for the same period
Discrepancies (difference > RM0) are flagged with a yellow warning showing the month, expected amount, and LHDN-credited amount
Reconciliation records are stored per assessment year and can be exported as a reconciliation report for audit purposes
System generates a monthly reminder alert on the 20th of each month prompting HR to verify e-Lejar balance against the previous month's CP39 payment
technicalNotes: e-Lejar is accessible at https://mytax.hasil.gov.my â€” employers log in with their employer TIN
e-Lejar shows up to 10 years of tax ledger history per LHDN information
No public API for e-Lejar programmatic access â€” integration is manual entry + comparison, not API pull
Reference: e-Lejar LHDN overview (https://ecentral.my/e-lejar-lhdn/)
dependencies: Build Monthly e-PCB Plus Submission Reconciliation View

userStories.title: Track Employment Act Section 60D Public Holiday Triple Pay and LHDN PCB Impact
userStories.description: Employment Act Section 60D mandates that employees working on a public holiday receive triple pay (3x ORP for work on a gazetted public holiday). This is already covered partially by the 'Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator' story, but the PCB/MTD treatment of the public holiday premium is not covered: the triple pay falls under Section 13(1)(a) employment income and is PCB-liable, must appear in EA Form, and impacts monthly MTD. Additionally, Section 60D covers the alternative of a replacement leave day â€” if replacement leave is taken instead of the extra pay, no additional PCB applies.
acceptanceCriteria: Public holiday premium pay (2x ORP top-up for working on a gazetted public holiday) is classified as regular employment income under Section 13(1)(a) ITA and included in the PCB base for that month
If the employer and employee agree to replacement leave instead of double premium, the system records the replacement leave grant and does not add taxable income
The public holiday work pay component is separately labelled in the EA Form under the applicable remuneration category
PCB calculation for months with public holiday work premium correctly inflates the gross remuneration for MTD purposes
System validates against a configurable list of Malaysian gazetted public holidays (federal + state) to correctly identify which days qualify
technicalNotes: EA Section 60D: employee working on paid public holiday entitled to: (a) ordinary rate + (b) day's wages at ORP = total 2x ORP extra pay (on top of basic), or replacement leave
The premium pay component is regular employment income (Section 13(1)(a) ITA) â€” subject to PCB
Replacement leave option avoids additional taxable income â€” must be documented
Reference: Employment Act 1955 Section 60D and employment law analysis (https://www.zulrafique.com.my)
dependencies: Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator (Section 60D Employment Act) with Replacement Leave Tracking
Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

userStories.title: PDPA 2024 Data Portability and Payroll Employee Data Export on Request
userStories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (gazetted and phased in Januaryâ€“June 2025) introduced a new data portability right: employees (data subjects) can request their personal data be transferred from the employer (data controller) to another party in a machine-readable format within a prescribed period. For payroll, this means employees can request an export of all their payroll data (payslips, EA forms, PCB deductions, leave records, statutory contribution history) in a portable format. The existing PDPA story covers DPO appointment and breach notification â€” this story covers the portability right specifically for payroll records.
acceptanceCriteria: An employee self-service action 'Request My Payroll Data Export' generates a structured export (JSON or CSV) of all the requesting employee's payroll records within the system
The export includes: all payslips, EA forms, TP1/TP2/TP3 records, EPF/SOCSO/EIS contributions per month, PCB deductions, and leave records for the current and prior 7 years
The export is generated within 7 days of request (system setting for response SLA) and is made available for download via a secure, time-limited link
A 'Data Subject Request Log' records who requested data, when, and when/how it was fulfilled â€” retained for audit
The export format is machine-readable (JSON preferred) and human-readable (PDF summary) â€” both generated simultaneously
technicalNotes: PDPA Amendment Act 2024 passed 31 July 2024; portability right phased in by June 2025
Portability right applies where processing is by automated means and based on consent or contract
Employment relationship creates a contract basis â€” payroll data qualifies for portability

Reference: Mayer Brown PDPA analysis (https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/07/from-legislative-reform-to-practical-guidance-key-amendments-to-malaysias-pdpa-and-the-launch-of-cross-border-transfer-guidelines)
dependencies: PDPA 2024 DPO Appointment Registry and 72-Hour Payroll Data Breach Notification Workflow
Build Employee Self-Service Portal for Payslips, EA Forms, and TP1 Submission

userStories.title: SOCSO Foreign Worker Invalidity Scheme Inclusion Flag and Rate Update (July 2024)
userStories.description: Effective July 1, 2024, PERKESO extended the SOCSO Invalidity Scheme to foreign workers under age 60, who were previously only covered under the Employment Injury Scheme alone. Employers must now contribute at the full First Category rate (Employment Injury + Invalidity combined) for eligible foreign workers, and the PERKESO ASSIST e-Caruman upload must use the correct scheme category code per employee. Any employer still remitting at the old employment-injury-only rate is under-contributing and faces PERKESO penalty exposure from the enforcement date.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType includes a socso_scheme_category Select field ('First Category (EI + Invalidity)' / 'Second Category (EI Only)'); foreign workers under age 60 hired on or after 2024-07-01 default to 'First Category'
PCB/payroll engine computes SOCSO contribution using the correct First Category rate for eligible foreign workers (combined EI + Invalidity scheme contribution amount from Jadual Kadar Caruman)
PERKESO ASSIST e-Caruman TXT upload uses correct scheme code per employee based on socso_scheme_category and age at payroll run date
