Existing foreign employees hired before July 2024 with blank or incorrect socso_scheme_category are flagged in a migration report for HR review
A hard validation warning fires on Salary Slip submission if a foreign worker under age 60 has socso_scheme_category set to 'Second Category'
technicalNotes: PERKESO official FAQ for foreign worker Invalidity Scheme extension: https://www.perkeso.gov.my/images/pekerja_asing/250724%20-FAQ%20-%20INVALIDITY%20SCHEME%20FOREIGN%20WORKER.pdf
First Category (under 60): employer 1.75%, employee 0.5% of insured wages â€” verify actual jadual amounts from https://www.perkeso.gov.my/en/rate-of-contribution.html
Second Category (60 and above): employer 1.25%, employee 0% â€” employment injury only, unchanged
Vialto Partners alert on the extension: https://vialtopartners.com/regional-alerts/malaysia-employment-tax-extension-of-invalidity-scheme-coverage-to-foreign-workers
Custom field: custom_socso_scheme_category on Employee DocType; trigger auto-assignment from nationality + date_of_birth + date_of_joining
dependencies: Generate SOCSO and EIS e-Caruman Upload File (PERKESO ASSIST Portal)

userStories.title: MTD Method 3 â€” PCB Computation for Weekly and Fortnightly Payroll Periods
userStories.description: The LHDN PCB specification defines three calculation methods: Method 1 (standard monthly), Method 2 (YTD recalculation), and Method 3 for employees paid on a non-monthly basis such as weekly or fortnightly cycles. Method 3 annualizes the periodic gross pay, computes annual MTD using the Schedule 1 formula, then converts back to the pay-period amount. Without this method, employers running weekly or fortnightly payroll have no compliant mechanism to determine the correct PCB per pay run, defaulting incorrectly to a monthly annualization that over- or under-deducts.
acceptanceCriteria: Payroll Entry and Payroll Period support pay_period_type (Monthly / Weekly / Fortnightly / Other) and pay_period_days fields

When pay_period_type is not Monthly, PCB engine applies Method 3: annualized_gross = periodic_gross Ã— (365 / pay_period_days); annual MTD computed from annualized amount less reliefs; period MTD = annual_MTD Ã— (pay_period_days / 365)
CP39 submission correctly aggregates multiple weekly/fortnightly runs within the same calendar month into a single monthly CP39 row per employee
EA Form correctly sums all pay-period MTDs for the assessment year totals regardless of pay frequency
Unit test: employee earning RM1,000 per week produces the same annual MTD as an equivalent monthly employee at RM4,333/month within a RM10 rounding tolerance
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB 2025 Specification PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
Method 3 is defined in the LHDN computerised MTD specification under 'Kaedah Pengiraan Untuk Tempoh Gaji Bukan Bulanan'
Monthly annualization: multiply weekly pay Ã— 52 or fortnightly Ã— 26; then divide annual MTD back by 52 or 26 for per-period deduction
CP39 month mapping: all weekly pay runs in e.g., March â†’ single CP39 March row; aggregate MTD and wages
Custom field on Payroll Period doctype: pay_period_type (Select); default 'Monthly' for backward compatibility
dependencies: Implement PCB/MTD monthly calculation validation
Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)

userStories.title: EPF i-Akaun Contribution File Whole Ringgit Rounding Rule Enforcement
userStories.description: KWSP mandates that all EPF contribution amounts must be paid and submitted in whole Ringgit Malaysia â€” no sen (cents) are accepted in the i-Akaun TXT/CSV upload file or during payment. The i-Akaun file generator must apply the correct rounding to both employer and employee contribution values before writing them to the file. Currently the system may generate decimal values causing file validation rejection by the KWSP portal, requiring HR to manually correct and re-upload the file each month.
acceptanceCriteria: EPF i-Akaun CSV/TXT file generator rounds each employee's employer-share and employee-share EPF amounts independently to the nearest whole Ringgit (standard arithmetic: â‰¥ 0.50 rounds up, < 0.50 truncates)
Voluntary excess contributions (i-Topup) are also rounded to whole Ringgit before file output
A per-payroll-run EPF Rounding Summary report shows sen absorbed or lost per employee line, with a batch total, for auditing
File upload to KWSP i-Akaun succeeds without format validation rejection due to decimal values in any contribution column
Unit test: employee salary RM3,333.33 â†’ employee EPF at 11% = RM366.667 â†’ file output RM367; employer at 13% = RM433.333 â†’ file output RM433
technicalNotes: KWSP official rule: 'Contributions must be paid in Ringgit Malaysia (RM) only, without cents, before the 15th of the following month'
Source: https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/employer/responsibilities/mandatory-contribution
Apply rounding at file-generation time only â€” payroll internal amounts must retain full precision (sens) for payslip accuracy and EPF balance reconciliation
The rounding sen difference is a legitimate cost/credit variance â€” it is NOT deducted additionally from the employee
Both the TXT (legacy) and CSV (current) i-Akaun formats require whole ringgit contribution columns
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File

userStories.title: MyInvois SDK v2.1 Compliance Validation for Payroll Self-Billed Invoice Submissions
userStories.description: On 6 April 2025, IRBM published updated MyInvois 2.1 guidelines with additional mandatory field requirements and clarifications on B2B transaction types including self-billed invoices â€” the mechanism used for payroll-related expense reimbursements. The payroll integration's XML builder and API client must be validated against the v2.1 specification to ensure continued acceptance by MyInvois without rejection. Any new mandatory fields, changed InvoiceTypeCode values, or updated schemeID vocabulary must be reflected in the builder.
acceptanceCriteria: All self-billed XML documents generated by the lhdn_payroll_integration pass LHDN MyInvois v2.1 schema validation with zero errors
Any new mandatory fields introduced in v2.1 (e.g., additional party identification, classification codes for payroll self-billing) are populated in the XML builder
API client uses the correct SDK v2.1 version-specific endpoint URLs and request headers as per the updated SDK documentation
Existing integration tests (test_intg_scenarios.py T-01 through T-05) continue to pass against v2.1 mock and sandbox responses without modification
The LHDN configuration DocType stores an sdk_version field set to '2.1' to make the active specification version auditable
technicalNotes: IRBM issued updated guidelines on 6 April 2025 â€” EY Malaysia tax alert: https://www.ey.com/en_my/technical/tax-alerts/the-inland-revenue-board-s-updated-guidelines-on-e-invoices-and-the-software-development-kit-july
Official MyInvois SDK documentation: https://sdk.myinvois.hasil.gov.my
Key areas to verify against v2.1: InvoiceTypeCode options for self-billing, PartyIdentification schemeID vocabulary, mandatory vs optional field changes, pagination/error response format changes
Self-billed invoices in payroll context: salary slips (self-billed by employer for employee remuneration) and expense claim reimbursements
dependencies: Wire build_consolidated_xml() into consolidation_service
Fix token expiry check and status poller base_url bug
Assess and Implement XAdES XML Digital Signature for MyInvois Self-Billed Phase 2

userStories.title: Retroactive Salary Arrears PCB Back-Period Spread Calculation
userStories.description: When employers award retroactive salary increases (back pay covering prior months within the same tax year), LHDN guidance allows the additional income to be spread across the months it was earned for MTD recalculation, avoiding a PCB spike in the month of payment. Currently the system treats all retroactive pay as additional income in the month paid (using the bonus/irregular pay pathway), which can cause significant over-deduction in a single month and employee hardship. A retroactive pay workflow that re-computes MTD for each affected prior month and generates corrected CP39 amendment rows is needed.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Slip includes custom_retroactive_amount, custom_retroactive_period_from, and custom_retroactive_period_to fields
When retroactive_amount > 0, system offers two modes: (a) lump-sum in current month (existing behaviour), or (b) back-period spread â€” the user selects

In back-period spread mode, system recomputes MTD for each affected prior month by adding proportional retroactive pay to that month's gross, recalculates tax, and collects the incremental MTD as retroactive_pcb_recovery in the current month's Salary Slip
CP39 file includes corrected prior-month rows (one per retroactive month) via the LHDN e-PCB Plus amendment submission format
EA Form classifies retroactive pay under the assessment year it was earned (not year paid) when both fall within the same calendar year
technicalNotes: LHDN guidance: retroactive pay covering prior months in same year may be spread across those months for MTD recalculation â€” reduces current-month PCB spike
Reference: Talenox LHDN PCB guide: https://blog.talenox.com/malaysia-tax-guide-how-do-i-calculate-pcb-mtd-part-2-of-3/
Cross-year retroactive pay (e.g., 2025 arrears paid in 2026) is taxed in year received â€” this edge case must be documented and handled separately
LHDN e-PCB Plus allows prior month amendment submissions â€” use this pathway for retroactive month CP39 corrections
This story complements 'e-PCB Plus Prior Month PCB Amendment' by providing the payroll-side calculation that drives what needs to be amended
dependencies: Generate CP39 monthly PCB remittance file
e-PCB Plus Prior Month PCB Amendment â€” Trigger Corrected CP39 Row on Salary Slip Resubmission

userStories.title: e-Invoice Mandatory Phase Readiness Check for Employer Company Records
userStories.description: LHDN's e-invoice mandate rolls out in phases based on annual turnover: Phase 1 (>RM100M, Aug 2024), Phase 2 (>RM25M, Jan 2025), Phase 3 (>RM150Kâ€“RM500K, July 2025), and Phase 4+ (all remaining businesses). Many employers using the payroll app are SMEs who crossed Phase 3 threshold in July 2025 and must now have MyInvois credentials configured. The payroll system should detect whether the employer's revenue tier requires active e-invoice compliance and warn HR if MyInvois API credentials are missing or the configuration is incomplete.
acceptanceCriteria: Company DocType includes custom_annual_revenue (Currency) and a read-only custom_einvoice_mandate_phase (Data) field auto-derived from revenue: '>RM100M (Phase 1)', '>RM25M (Phase 2)', '>RM150K (Phase 3)', 'All Businesses (Phase 4+)', or 'Below RM150K (Voluntary)'

On Company save, if einvoice_mandate_phase is not 'Below RM150K' and custom_client_id or custom_client_secret is blank, a prominent warning banner is shown: 'e-Invoice mandatory for this company â€” MyInvois API credentials are missing'
An e-Invoice Readiness Report lists all Companies in the system, their mandate phase, and compliance status (Compliant / Credentials Missing / Voluntary / Not Applicable)
Alert email is sent to HR Admin when a Company's custom_annual_revenue change moves it into a new mandatory phase
The readiness check is a pre-flight guard only â€” it does not prevent payroll runs but flags the MyInvois gap for action
technicalNotes: LHDN e-Invoice phase schedule: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/e-invoice/
Phase 3 (from July 2025): taxpayers with annual revenue RM150Kâ€“RM500K are now mandatory
Phase 4/5 (all remaining taxpayers with any turnover): LHDN has not finalized the date for the final phase â€” configure as 'TBD' or rely on LHDN announcement
This check complements the existing myinvois_erpgulf app setup â€” it validates the payroll employer configuration, not the e-invoice submission flow itself

userStories.title: Mid-Year TP1 Relief Amendment PCB Recalculation and Adjustment Schedule
userStories.description: When an employee submits an amended TP1 form mid-year (e.g., adding a new dependent, claiming a new relief category, or correcting a relief amount), the MTD calculation for all remaining months of the year must be recalculated to reflect the updated annual tax liability. Without a structured recalculation workflow, HR must manually adjust PCB in subsequent Salary Slips â€” a process prone to errors and omissions. This story implements a TP1 amendment detection, recalculation, and schedule-approval workflow that automates the MTD adjustment across remaining payroll months.
acceptanceCriteria: When a TP1 form's status changes to 'Amended' or a new TP1 is submitted mid-year, a 'PCB Recalculation Pending' flag is set on the employee's active payroll record

HR can trigger a 'Recalculate Remaining MTD' action that computes: revised annual MTD using updated TP1 relief totals, less MTD already deducted YTD, and spreads the remainder equally across remaining months
The system generates a PCB Adjustment Schedule (tabular: month, old MTD, new MTD, difference) that HR must review and approve before it applies to future Salary Slips
Upon approval, subsequent Salary Slips for that employee automatically use the revised monthly MTD without manual intervention
An audit log entry records: TP1 submission date, previous total reliefs, new total reliefs, MTD YTD at change date, revised monthly MTD, and approving HR user
technicalNotes: MTD recalculation formula: Revised_monthly_MTD = (Revised_annual_MTD - MTD_already_deducted_YTD) / remaining_payroll_months_in_year

Edge case: revised annual MTD < MTD already deducted (overpayment) â†’ employee gets reduced or zero MTD for remaining months; refund via payroll is NOT standard â€” employee must claim via annual tax filing
TP1(1/2026) updates reference: https://support.briohr.com/knowledge/payroll-malaysia-key-tax-relief-updates-for-the-year-2026
LHDN employer payroll data specification: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/employer-payroll-data-specification/
This story is operationally distinct from 'Alert on Stale TP1 Relief Declarations' â€” that story alerts at year start; this story handles in-year amendments and drives MTD recalculation
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration
Alert on Stale TP1 Relief Declarations When New Assessment Year Begins Without Employee Re-Submission

userStories.title: Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA) Prescribed Value Calculation Module for EA Form Part C
userStories.description: LHDN Public Ruling PR 3/2005 (on Value of Living Accommodation) requires employer-provided housing to be declared as taxable employment income on EA Form Part C. The taxable VOLA is calculated using either: (a) Formula Method: Defined Rate Ã— Annual Value of Accommodation, capped at 30% of the employee's gross annual employment income; or (b) actual rent paid by employer. Without this module, employers providing company housing or housing subsidies are unable to correctly populate Part C of the EA Form and underreport taxable BIK to LHDN. This is distinct from the general BIK module (which covers motor vehicles) and the VOLA election via TP2.
acceptanceCriteria: A VOLA configuration section on Employee or its annual payroll record captures: accommodation_type (Employer-Owned / Employer-Rented / Employee-Rented-with-Subsidy), annual_property_value, actual_annual_rent_paid_by_employer, employee_rent_contribution_annual, and days_occupied
For Employer-Owned: VOLA = min(Defined Rate Ã— annual_value Ã— days/365, 30% of annual gross income from employment)
For Employer-Rented: VOLA = actual annual rent paid by employer (no formula cap applies)
For Subsidized: VOLA = employer-paid portion only (actual rent minus employee contribution)
EA Form Part C (accommodation sub-section) is auto-populated with the computed VOLA amount from the above calculation
Unit test: employee gross RM60,000/year; employer-owned property annual value RM24,000; defined rate 30%: formula = 30% Ã— RM24,000 = RM7,200; 30% cap = RM18,000; VOLA = RM7,200 (formula lower)
technicalNotes: LHDN Public Ruling PR 3/2005 'Value of Living Accommodation' â€” primary authority for VOLA calculation
LHDN BIK Public Ruling 3/2013 (covers VOLA and perquisites): https://phl.hasil.gov.my/pdf/pdfam/PR3_2013.pdf
Defined Rate per LHDN for accommodation: varies by accommodation type and annual value â€” verify from latest LHDN Public Ruling
VOLA is declared in EA Form Part C (separate from BIK motor vehicle which is Part C motor vehicle sub-section)
This module extends 'Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module' specifically for the living accommodation category
Talenox VOLA guide: https://help.talenox.com/en/articles/8290345-what-is-the-value-of-living-accommodation-vola-malaysia
dependencies: Rebuild EA Form to Include All Mandatory LHDN Section A/B/C/D Fields
Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

userStories.title: Progressive Wage Policy Government Incentive Subsidy Claim Tracking Workflow
userStories.description: The Progressive Wage Policy (PWP), expanded in 2025 with RM200 million budget, provides participating employers a government incentive of RM300 per eligible employee per month for up to 12 months. Eligible employees must earn between RM1,500 and RM4,999/month and have at least 12 months' service. Participating companies must provide skill-development training as a condition. The payroll system currently tracks only the PWP eligibility flag but has no workflow for subsidy claim application submission, per-employee entitlement tracking, or alert when the 12-month subsidy claim window nears expiry.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType includes custom_pwp_enrolled (boolean), custom_pwp_enrollment_date, custom_pwp_approval_reference (from MOHR portal), and custom_pwp_subsidy_months_claimed (0â€“12)
PWP Eligibility Report auto-identifies employees earning RM1,500â€“RM4,999 with â‰¥12 months service and flags those not yet enrolled for HR action
Monthly PWP Claim Schedule report generates a summary: enrolled employee count, RM300 Ã— months per employee, cumulative total subsidy entitlement vs claimed to date
An alert fires when a PWP-enrolled employee's salary revision takes them above RM4,999 (exits the subsidy band) or when their 12-month claim window is within 2 months of expiry
Training Evidence checkbox on Employee allows HR to mark that the required skill training evidence has been recorded as a condition of PWP participation
technicalNotes: PWP is administered by MOHR / TalentCorp â€” employers apply via the MOHR portal and receive an approval reference
Latest expansion: RM200 million budget targeting 50,000 workers, salary increase from RM1,900 to RM2,200 at entry level confirmed as of Dec 2025
Source: Malay Mail report (23 Feb 2026): https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/02/23/pay-rises-on-the-way-progressive-wage-policy-to-boost-salaries-for-over-50000-workers-by-2027/210097
This story extends 'Progressive Wage Policy (PWP) Eligibility Flag and Wage Escalation Compliance Report' with the claim-tracking operational workflow
dependencies: Progressive Wage Policy (PWP) Eligibility Flag and Wage Escalation Compliance Report

userStories.title: SOCSO Act Amendment 2025 â€” Remove Working-Hours Restriction from Employment Injury Claims and Borang 34
userStories.description: The Employees' Social Security Act (Amendment) Bill 2025 (Act 4) was passed by Parliament on 2 December 2025 and gazetted in December 2025, extending SOCSO employment injury coverage to 24 hours per day regardless of when or where an accident occurs. The previous working-hours restriction caused 12,306 valid claims (2023â€“Oct 2025) to be rejected as non-work-related. Payroll and HR systems must remove any working-hours gate from Borang 34 accident notification workflows and update SOCSO coverage scope labels on employee records.
acceptanceCriteria: Borang 34 accident notification form no longer restricts 'time of accident' to standard working hours or requires a work-location checkbox to proceed
Employee SOCSO record displays '24-Hour Employment Injury Coverage (effective Dec 2025)' as the coverage scope label
Existing Borang 34 records created before the amendment retain their original data; no automated backfill migration runs
An HR dashboard banner notifies users of the coverage expansion with the effective date and a link to the PERKESO official announcement
technicalNotes: Act 4 removes the 'during working hours' restriction from the definition of 'employment injury' in the Employees' Social Security Act 1969 (Act 4)
No change to SOCSO contribution rates or contribution table â€” this is a coverage-scope change only
Affects the Borang 34 (48-hour accident notification) workflow field validation: time_of_accident must accept any hour of the day
PERKESO press release and Act 4 gazette reference from businesstoday.com.my Dec 2025
dependencies: Generate PERKESO Borang 34 Accident Notification Within 48-Hour Statutory Deadline

userStories.title: EIS Amendment 2025 â€” Payroll Validation: Prohibit Employer EIS Contribution Recovery from Employee Wages
userStories.description: The EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 explicitly prohibits employers from recovering their share of EIS contributions from employee wages, clarifying that only the employee's share (0.2% capped at RM6,000 wage ceiling) may appear as a deduction on payslips. Some employers had been improperly charging the employer's 0.4% EIS share to employees. The payroll engine must enforce a validation rule that rejects any salary deduction component tagged as employer-side EIS recovery.
acceptanceCriteria: Payroll validation raises a blocking error if any salary deduction component has both custom_is_eis_component = 1 and is_employer_contribution = 1
The LHDN Payroll Compliance Report shows separate rows for employer EIS (0.4%) and employee EIS (0.2%) with column 'Recoverable from Employee?' = No / Yes respectively
Existing salary structures containing employer EIS deduction components are flagged in a pre-migration report for HR review
Unit test asserts that a Salary Slip with an incorrectly added employer EIS deduction component raises a validation exception before submission
technicalNotes: EIS rates: employer 0.4%, employee 0.2% â€” both capped at RM6,000 wage ceiling (effective Oct 2024)
EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 reinforces that only the employee share may be deducted from salary â€” employer share is a company cost
Salary Component doctype: check the combination of custom_is_eis_component and is_employer_contribution to detect illegal cross-recovery
This rule applies equally to the SOCSO employer contribution share (already prohibited under SOCSO Act)
dependencies: Generate SOCSO and EIS e-Caruman Upload File (PERKESO ASSIST Portal)

userStories.title: MyInvois Phase 4 (January 2026) â€” Set Mandatory e-Invoice Flag for Companies with RM1Mâ€“RM5M Annual Revenue
userStories.description: Effective 1 January 2026, Phase 4 of Malaysia's MyInvois mandate requires all businesses with annual turnover between RM1 million and RM5 million to comply with mandatory e-invoicing, including self-billed salary slip e-invoices where payroll qualifies as a self-billing scenario. The Company DocType needs a revenue band field so the system can conditionally enable mandatory self-billed e-invoice generation for salary slips and alert HR if MyInvois credentials are not configured.
acceptanceCriteria: Company DocType has custom_annual_revenue_band (Select: <RM1M | RM1M-RM5M | RM5M-RM25M | RM25M-RM100M | >RM100M) and computed custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase (Exempt | Phase4 | Phase3 | Phase2 | Phase1)
When custom_annual_revenue_band = 'RM1M-RM5M', the system enables mandatory self-billed e-invoice generation for applicable payroll documents from 2026-01-01
If custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase is not 'Exempt' and MyInvois API credentials (custom_client_id / custom_client_secret) are blank, a warning banner is displayed on Company page
An e-Invoice Readiness Report lists all companies with their mandate phase and compliance status (Configured / Credentials Missing / Exempt / Voluntary)
technicalNotes: Phase 4 effective: 1 January 2026 for RM1Mâ€“RM5M turnover; relaxation period ends 30 June 2026
Phase 5 (previously RM500Kâ€“RM1M, due July 2026) is cancelled â€” threshold raised to RM1M (Cabinet decision 6 Dec 2025)
This story extends the existing 'e-Invoice Mandatory Phase Readiness Check' with the specific Phase 4 revenue band and conditional payroll enforcement logic
LHDN e-Invoice Implementation FAQ: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/0xqitc2t/lhdnm-e-invoice-general-faqs.pdf
dependencies: e-Invoice Mandatory Phase Readiness Check for Employer Company Records
MyInvois SDK v2.1 Compliance Validation for Payroll Self-Billed Invoice Submissions

userStories.title: MyInvois RM1M Exemption Threshold Update â€” Revoke Mandatory Flag for Sub-RM1M Companies and Cancel Phase 5
userStories.description: On 6 December 2025, the Malaysian Cabinet approved raising the e-invoice exemption threshold from RM500,000 to RM1,000,000 in annual turnover and cancelled the previously planned Phase 5 rollout (which was due July 2026 for RM500Kâ€“RM1M businesses). Any Company records previously configured as Phase 5 mandatory must be reclassified as Voluntarily Exempt to prevent these employers from being incorrectly required to generate self-billed e-invoices for salary slips.
acceptanceCriteria: A one-time data migration sets custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase = 'Exempt' for all companies with custom_annual_revenue_band = '<RM1M'
The migration log captures how many company records were reclassified and notifies the system administrator
LHDN Payroll Compliance Report no longer shows a Phase 5 deadline row for sub-RM1M companies
A notice in the LHDN Payroll Workspace informs users that Phase 5 has been cancelled as of December 2025 and sub-RM1M companies are voluntarily exempt
technicalNotes: Cabinet decision date: 6 December 2025 (confirmed by Comarch, VATupdate, Sovos)
Around 200,000 additional micro and small businesses now fall under this voluntary exemption
Phase 5 previously covered: RM500Kâ€“RM1M turnover, mandatory from 1 July 2026 â€” this phase is now cancelled
Businesses below RM1M may still adopt e-invoicing voluntarily but face no penalties for not doing so
dependencies: MyInvois Phase 4 (January 2026) â€” Set Mandatory e-Invoice Flag for Companies with RM1Mâ€“RM5M Annual Revenue

userStories.title: PCB 2026 MTD Specification â€” Automated Test Suite Validating Calculator Against LHDN Amendment Spec PDF
userStories.description: LHDN published the 'Amendment to: Specification for Computerised Calculation Method of MTD 2026' in 2025, detailing updated TP1 deduction items and any formula cross-checks specific to YA2026. While the existing 'Generate LHDN PCB 2026 Specification Annual Update Checklist' tracks what changed, no automated test suite validates that the PCB calculator's actual numeric output matches the LHDN specification's own worked examples. LHDN-approved software registration requires compliance with the specification â€” bit-exact test coverage is the auditable proof.
acceptanceCriteria: A test module test_mtd_spec_2026.py contains at least 10 test cases extracted from the LHDN 2026 MTD specification PDF, covering Method 1 (annual projection), Method 2 (YTD), and the new TP1(1/2026) relief items
Each test asserts the PCB calculator output matches the LHDN reference value within RM0.01 (rounding tolerance per the 5-cent-ceiling rule already implemented)
Tests cover edge cases: employees claiming new YA2026 life insurance (children extension), EV charging relief, and the Budget 2026 TP1 changes
CI pipeline runs test_mtd_spec_2026.py on every PR that touches the PCB calculation module; failures block the PR merge
A version field custom_mtd_spec_version ('2026') on LHDN Payroll Settings records which specification the calculator was last certified against
technicalNotes: LHDN 2026 MTD Spec PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf
Key 2026 changes: TP1 life insurance extended to children; Budget 2026 new TP1 items; truncation-then-5-cent-ceiling rounding (already in PRD as US-202)
LHDN approved software provider list (updated 22 Sep 2025): compliance with current spec is a prerequisite for vendor listing at hasil.gov.my/en/employers/list-of-software-provider-or-employer/
Extract worked examples from the PDF as test fixtures â€” use parameterized pytest for each example row
dependencies: PCB 2026 Specification: Enforce Truncation-then-5-Cent-Ceiling Rounding Rule in MTD Calculation Engine
Generate LHDN PCB 2026 Specification Annual Update Checklist and Version Flag

userStories.title: LHDN AIS Employer Upload â€” Auto-Submit Employee Remuneration Data to MyTax by 25 February Deadline
userStories.description: LHDN's Auto Income Submission (AIS) system requires employers to upload employee remuneration data to the MyTax employer portal by 25 February each year so that employees' ITRF e-Filing forms can be pre-populated automatically. This is distinct from Form E (deadline 31 March) and EA Form distribution (deadline 28 February). Non-submission of AIS data means employees cannot use the LHDN pre-filled e-Filing service and must enter all income data manually. The payroll system should generate the AIS upload file and track submission status.
acceptanceCriteria: A report 'LHDN AIS Employee Remuneration Export' generates the AIS data file for all active employees for the selected tax year, covering: employee income tax number, IC/passport, total gross employment income, EPF deduction, PCB deducted, bonus, allowances, and BIK
The compliance calendar displays AIS submission deadline as 25 February, listed separately from Form E (31 March) and EA Form distribution (28 February)
After submission, HR can mark 'AIS Submitted' with a submission reference number; the system records this and clears the pre-deadline alert
An alert email is sent to HR Admin on 20 February (5-day warning) if AIS submission status is 'Not Submitted' for any company
The export file format matches the LHDN employer data specification for AIS â€” field order and data types as specified by LHDN MyTax portal guidance
technicalNotes: LHDN AIS portal: accessible via MyTax employer login; employers encouraged to upload by 25 February for employee ITRF pre-fill
AIS data is a superset of EA Form data â€” reuse the EA Form data extraction logic with additional fields required by AIS format
AIS is separate from e-Data PCB (monthly MTD), Form E/CP8D (annual return), and EA Form (individual employee distribution)
Source: LHDN employer guides at hasil.gov.my/en/employers/ and QuickHR LHDN compliance guide
dependencies: Generate EA Form (Borang EA) for Employees
Generate Borang E (Form E) Employer Annual Return

userStories.title: EIS Amendment 2025 (S.45F) â€” Employer Job Vacancy Pre-Notification and 7-Day Post-Filling PERKESO Alert
userStories.description: The Employment Insurance System (Amendment) Bill 2025, tabled in Parliament on 4 November 2025, introduces Section 45F requiring employers to notify PERKESO in writing about a job vacancy before hiring and again within 7 days after the position is filled. Non-compliance carries a fine of up to RM10,000 per conviction. A 2-year moratorium will precede enforcement and SMEs are under consultation for a possible exemption, but large employers should begin preparing the workflow now.
acceptanceCriteria: The New Employee onboarding checklist includes a 'Notify PERKESO of filled vacancy (S.45F)' step with due_date = date_of_joining + 7 days
A compliance alert fires if the PERKESO notification step is skipped and more than 7 days have elapsed since the employee's date_of_joining
Company Settings has a flag custom_eis_s45f_applicable (default ON for non-SME; shows a 'SME exemption pending' warning when toggled OFF)
When the flag is ON, the system generates a pre-formatted PERKESO Vacancy Filled Notification letter PDF with employee details, vacancy title, and filling date
technicalNotes: EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 â€” Dewan Rakyat first reading 4 November 2025; Section 45F is the new job vacancy notification provision
Two-year moratorium before enforcement penalties apply; SME exemption under stakeholder consultation with PERKESO
Penalty for non-compliance: fine not exceeding RM10,000 on conviction
The Malay Mail (6 Dec 2025) reported that SOCSO plans to exempt SMEs from mandatory vacancy reporting

userStories.title: EIS Amendment 2025 â€” Display Updated Statutory EIS Benefit Rates on Employee Termination Workflow
userStories.description: The EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 proposes increasing the maximum training fee claimable from RM4,000 to RM7,000 and raising the re-employment placement allowance from 25% to 50% of the employee's last drawn salary. When processing employee termination in payroll, the system currently shows outdated EIS benefit rates. These must be updated to help HR accurately communicate the enhanced EIS entitlements available to retrenched employees, supporting their reemployment journey.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee termination form displays an EIS Benefit Reference panel showing: job search allowance rate, re-employment allowance (50% of last salary), training allowance (up to RM30/day), and maximum training fee (RM7,000)
Panel shows an 'Effective Date' label tied to the EIS Amendment 2025 gazette date (to be updated when gazetted)
EIS Benefit rates are stored in a configurable EIS Benefit Rate Settings DocType (not hardcoded) so they can be updated via fixture when the Amendment is formally gazetted
A banner on the Termination form links to the PERKESO ASSIST portal for the employee to initiate their EIS claim
technicalNotes: EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 tabled 4 November 2025 â€” rates are proposed, not yet gazetted as of research date; implement with a 'pending gazette' flag
Current (pre-amendment) rates: training fee max RM4,000; re-employment allowance 25% of salary; training allowance unspecified
Proposed new rates: training fee max RM7,000; re-employment allowance 50% of salary; training allowance RM30/day
Job search allowance is a percentage of last salary paid over a sliding time window based on months of EIS contribution â€” display the rate table

userStories.title: HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert â€” Pre-Warning Before Accumulated Levy Is Lost
userStories.description: HRD Corp's policy states that if an employer makes no training claims for 2 years from initial registration or from the date of the last approved claim, the levy accumulated during that 2-year period is forfeited (HRD Corp retains up to RM10,000 from the remaining balance). The system currently alerts on the 15% under-utilisation penalty but does not separately track the 2-year forfeiture deadline. An 18-month inactivity warning allows HR to take action â€” submit a claim, arrange training, or flag the situation to management â€” before the forfeiture date.
acceptanceCriteria: LHDN Payroll Compliance Report includes a 'HRD Corp Levy Forfeiture Risk' row showing: last claim date, months since last claim, and projected forfeiture date (24 months after last approved claim or HRD Corp registration date)

A monthly scheduled job creates a High Priority Notification if months_since_last_claim >= 18, alerting HR Manager with the message: 'HRD Corp levy accrued since [date] is at forfeiture risk on [date]. Submit a training claim before this date to avoid loss of levy funds.'
Company HRDF Settings stores hrd_last_claim_date that HR can update manually when a claim is submitted externally
The alert is separate from the 15% under-utilisation alert â€” both can be active simultaneously
technicalNotes: HRD Corp forfeiture rule: no training claim in 2 years from registration or last financial assistance â†’ levy for those 2 years is forfeited; HRD Corp retains up to RM10,000 from balance
This is distinct from the 15% under-utilisation penalty (which applies to annual unclaimed levy as a percentage of total levy paid)
Source: HRD Corp FAQ at https://hrdcorp.gov.my/faq
Enforce alert threshold at 18 months to give HR a 6-month window before the 24-month forfeiture triggers
dependencies: Track HRD Corp Levy Utilization Rate and Alert on 15% Under-Utilisation Penalty

userStories.title: CP500 Erroneous Issuance Warning â€” Alert HR When a Pure-Salary Employee Has Received CP500
userStories.description: In December 2025 and January 2026, LHDN issued CP500 (bi-monthly advance tax installment) notices to thousands of salaried employees who had no non-employment income, due to incorrect income classification in their ITRF filings. LHDN confirmed no penalties for 2026 but employees must submit CP502 to cancel or amend the erroneous notice. Payroll systems can assist by detecting employees with 100% employment income (all PCB-deducted) who have been flagged as CP500 recipients, and by generating a corrective CP502 letter template.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType has fields custom_cp500_received (Checkbox) and a computed read-only field custom_cp500_applicable (True only if employee has declared non-employment income on TP1/TP2)

When custom_cp500_received is ticked for an employee where custom_cp500_applicable = False, the system displays a warning: 'This employee appears to have only employment income (PCB deducted). CP500 may be erroneous. Generate CP502 amendment letter for the employee.'

A 'Generate CP502 Amendment Letter' button on the employee record produces a PDF pre-filled with: employee name, income tax reference number, and a declaration that income is solely from employment subject to monthly PCB deduction
A Payroll Report 'CP500 Status Check' lists all employees with cp500_received = True and highlights those where cp500_applicable = False as requiring follow-up
technicalNotes: CP500 is issued to taxpayers with business, rental, royalty, or dividend income; pure-salaried employees should not receive it
LHDN clarification (Dec 2025 / Jan 2026): the CP500 issuance was due to incorrect ITRF income classification by the employee â€” not an LHDN system error
CP502 form: Application for Amendment of Instalment Payments â€” deadline for first revision: 30 June; second revision: 31 October
LHDN confirmed no penalties for CP500 non-compliance in 2026 due to the erroneous issuance â€” source: Malay Mail 5 Jan 2026
dependencies: CP500 Bi-Monthly Advance Tax Installment Awareness Panel for Directors with Non-Salary Income

userStories.title: Employment Act Amendment 2025 â€” Sexual Harassment Mandatory 30-Day Investigation Tracker with RM50,000 Penalty Alert
userStories.description: Amendments to the Employment Act (effective 2023 with 2025 penalty increase) and the Penal Code (effective July 2025, criminalising workplace bullying) require employers to investigate every sexual harassment complaint within 30 days and inform the complainant of findings in writing. Failure to investigate now carries a penalty of up to RM50,000 (increased from RM10,000). A complaint-to-outcome tracker in the payroll/HR system ensures HR Managers are alerted before the 30-day deadline lapses.
acceptanceCriteria: A new DocType 'Workplace Harassment Complaint' captures: complaint_date, complainant (link: Employee), respondent (link: Employee), investigation_due_date (auto: complaint_date + 30 calendar days), outcome_date, status (Open | Under Investigation | Completed | Referred to Tribunal)
A daily scheduled job creates a High Priority ToDo for HR Manager when status = 'Under Investigation' and today > investigation_due_date, including the RM50,000 penalty warning
When the complainant or respondent is placed on paid suspension during investigation, a Salary Slip remark 'Investigation Suspension Pay' is appended automatically
An HR report 'Open Harassment Complaints' lists all active complaints with days elapsed, days remaining, and the responsible HR Manager
technicalNotes: Legal basis: Employment Act 1955 Sections 81H (display notice) and 81I (mandatory investigation), amended 2022 in force Jan 2023; penalty increased to RM50,000 by 2025 amendment
Penal Code amendment effective 11 July 2025 adds workplace bullying as a criminal offense under amended Section 509
Anti-Sexual Harassment Act 2022 (ASHA) creates a separate tribunal avenue independent of EA internal investigation
Employment Act Section 81H requires employers to display an awareness notice â€” system can generate and track the notice posting date as a compliance record

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1 â€” EV Charging Facility Relief (RM2,500) as a Distinct Line Item Extended to YA2027
userStories.description: The RM2,500 individual tax relief for EV charging facility expenses (installation, rental, hire-purchase, or subscription fees) is extended through YA2027. This is a distinct relief from the general RM2,500 lifestyle relief (which covers computers, phones, CCTV, and food waste disposers). Employees who declare EV charging costs must have a separate TP1 field to prevent double-counting against the lifestyle relief cap. The LHDN TP1(1/2026) form lists EV charging as a separate claimable item.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration DocType has a field custom_ev_charging_relief (Currency, max RM2,500) that is distinct from the custom_lifestyle_relief field
PCB calculation applies EV charging relief as a deductible item independent of the lifestyle relief cap â€” an employee can claim both RM2,500 lifestyle AND RM2,500 EV charging in the same year
EA Form Part C shows EV charging relief under its correct LHDN section code matching the TP1(1/2026) layout
Unit test: employee claiming full RM2,500 lifestyle + full RM2,500 EV charging = RM5,000 total deductible, applied correctly in MTD calculation
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 extended EV charging facility relief: YA2022â€“YA2027
Eligible: installation cost, rental fee, hire-purchase equipment cost, subscription fees for EV charging
LHDN TP1(1/2026) specification PDF at https://www.hasil.gov.my â€” verify exact section placement for EV charging vs lifestyle relief
This is separate from the RM2,500 eco-friendly lifestyle items (CCTV, food waste disposer) added in Budget 2026
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60 Rest Day Pay â€” Enforce RM4,000 Wage Threshold and Tiered Rate Calculation
userStories.description: The Employment (Amendment) Act 2022 (effective 1 January 2023) restricts mandatory rest day pay entitlements to employees earning RM4,000 per month or below. Above this threshold, rest day pay is governed by contract only. The payroll system must enforce this threshold during Salary Slip computation: employees above RM4,000 should not receive statutory rest day pay automatically, and the three-tier rate (0.5x ORP / 1.0x ORP / 2.0x hourly rate) must be correctly applied for eligible employees. Currently the system applies rest day pay without the wage threshold check.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Slip computation applies statutory rest day pay rates (0.5x ORP for â‰¤half normal hours; 1.0x ORP for >half but â‰¤normal hours; 2.0x hourly rate for overtime beyond normal hours on rest day) only when employee monthly wage â‰¤ RM4,000

When employee wage > RM4,000 and a rest day work component is added, the system displays a warning: 'Statutory rest day pay (S.60 EA) does not apply to this employee. Use contractual rate instead.' and blocks the statutory component
Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) divisor is 26 for monthly-rated employees; the computed ORP and resulting rest day component value are shown on the Salary Slip detail panel
A payroll audit report 'Rest Day Pay Compliance Check' lists employees who received rest day pay in the selected period and flags those above RM4,000 monthly salary for HR review
Unit test: employee at RM3,999 receives statutory rest day pay; employee at RM4,001 does not, and a warning is logged
technicalNotes: Employment (Amendment) Act 2022 Section 60(3): overtime/rest day pay provisions excluded for employees above RM4,000/month
Three-tier rest day pay rates per S.60(3): work â‰¤ half normal hours = 0.5 day ORP; work > half but â‰¤ normal hours = 1 day ORP; work beyond normal hours = 2x hourly rate
ORP divisor: monthly salary Ã· 26 (Employment Act S.60I) â€” do not use 30 or calendar days
Manual workers (defined in EA S.2) are entitled to rest day pay regardless of wage â€” add an is_manual_worker flag on Employee to override the RM4,000 threshold
dependencies: Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1 â€” Extend Medical Relief to Grandparents and Add Vaccination Sub-Limit for Parents
userStories.description: Budget 2026 expands the scope of Malaysia's medical expense tax relief. Grandparents are now eligible under the RM8,000 medical care relief (previously restricted to parents). Additionally, within the existing RM10,000 total medical relief cap, taxpayers may now include vaccination costs for parents (not just full medical check-ups). Employees who declare these reliefs on TP1 will reduce their monthly PCB deduction. The TP1 Declaration DocType must accommodate the grandparent category and separate vaccination sub-limit for PCB computation accuracy.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration DocType adds custom_medical_grandparents_relief (Currency, max RM8,000) separate from the existing custom_medical_parents_relief field
TP1 Declaration DocType adds custom_vaccination_parents_relief (Currency, max RM1,000) as a sub-field within custom_medical_parents_relief (both count toward the RM10,000 combined medical relief cap)
PCB calculation sums grandparent + parent + vaccination medical reliefs and caps the total at RM10,000 before applying to the tax computation
EA Form Part C correctly reflects the grandparent and vaccination relief sub-categories using the LHDN TP1(1/2026) section codes
Unit test: employee claiming RM8,000 grandparents + RM1,000 parent vaccination + RM1,000 parent check-up = RM10,000 total; system caps at RM10,000 and does not allow an additional RM1,000
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 change: grandparents now included in medical care relief (RM8,000) â€” previously only parents were eligible
Vaccination costs for parents now claimable under the RM1,000 full medical check-up sub-limit within the RM10,000 cap
LHDN TP1(1/2026) form published at hasil.gov.my â€” verify exact section code and cap structure in the official form
This story is distinct from the already-covered 'Budget 2026 TP1: Expand Vaccination Relief Scope to All NPRA-Approved Vaccines' which covers the employee's own vaccination relief
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: EPF Automatic Foreign Worker i-Akaun Registration (October 2025) â€” Suppress Manual Registration for EP/VP Holders
userStories.description: Effective October 2025, EPF automatically registers non-Malaysian citizen employees who hold a Visitor's Pass (Temporary Employment) or Employment Pass directly upon employer contribution submission â€” eliminating the need for employees to visit EPF offices or for employers to manually register them via i-Akaun. The payroll system currently prompts HR to manually trigger EPF registration for new foreign hires. This workflow must be updated so that for EP and VP holders hired from October 2025 onwards, the system relies on EPF's auto-registration and only alerts if the employee's pass type is a work permit (which still requires manual registration).
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType custom_pass_type field (Select: Employment Pass | Visitor Pass (Temporary Employment) | Work Permit | Malaysia PR | Citizen | Other) is used to differentiate auto-registration eligibility

For employees with custom_pass_type = 'Employment Pass' or 'Visitor Pass (Temporary Employment)' hired from 2025-10-01 onwards, the new employee onboarding checklist shows 'EPF Auto-Registration (via contribution submission)' â€” no manual registration step required
For employees with custom_pass_type = 'Work Permit', the onboarding checklist retains the 'Register EPF Member â€” i-Akaun' manual step with a 7-day deadline alert

An informational banner on the foreign worker EPF section states: 'EP and VP holders (from Oct 2025): EPF membership is auto-registered when first contribution is submitted. Verify employee details on i-Akaun (Employer) after the first contribution month.'
Unit test: new EP employee hired Nov 2025 does not generate a manual EPF registration ToDo; Work Permit employee does
technicalNotes: KWSP announcement: 'Automatic Registration Eases Process For Employers Of Non-Malaysian Citizen Employees' (Oct 2025) â€” https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/w/news/automatic-registration-eases-process-for-employers-of-non-malaysian-citizen-employees
Auto-registration applies to Employment Pass and Visitor's Pass (Temporary Employment) holders; Work Permit holders still require manual employer-initiated registration
Employer must still verify the EPF member number appears correctly on i-Akaun (Employer) after the first contribution submission
This story is distinct from the existing '30-Day EPF i-Akaun Registration Deadline Alert for Newly Eligible Foreign Workers' which covers the October 2025 mandatory contribution requirement
dependencies: Enforce 30-Day EPF i-Akaun Registration Deadline Alert for Newly Eligible Foreign Workers (Oct 2025)

userStories.title: CP38 Additional Deduction â€” Include in e-PCB Plus Monthly Upload Alongside CP39
userStories.description: The e-PCB Plus system (launched 3 February 2025) integrates CP39 (monthly PCB) and CP38 (additional tax deduction orders) under a single MyTax employer portal. Previously, CP38 payments were made separately via the ByrHASiL portal. Employers must now ensure that CP38 deduction amounts are included in the monthly e-PCB Plus submission data alongside the normal CP39 PCB figures. The payroll system generates the CP39 upload file but does not currently append CP38 data. Both must be submitted by the 15th of the following month.
acceptanceCriteria: The monthly PCB submission file generator includes CP38 deduction rows for any employee who has an active CP38 Tax Deduction Order with amount > 0 for the payroll period
CP38 and CP39 rows in the upload file are distinguishable by their transaction type code (per LHDN e-PCB Plus file format specification)
The LHDN Payroll Compliance Report shows a combined 'e-PCB Plus Monthly Submission' row with sub-totals for CP39 (normal PCB) and CP38 (additional deductions) separately

A warning alert fires if an employee has a CP38 order active but the payroll module generated no CP38 row in the upload (e.g., zero salary month) â€” HR must be notified to handle the missed deduction
Unit test: employee with active CP38 order of RM200 and normal PCB of RM500 produces an upload file with two rows â€” one CP39 row (RM500) and one CP38 row (RM200) for that employee
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus launched 3 February 2025 on MyTax portal â€” replaces separate e-CP39, e-Data PCB, and ByrHASiL CP38 portals
CP38 = additional tax deduction order issued by LHDN to recover outstanding tax balance via employer payroll deductions
Payment deadline for both CP39 and CP38: 15th of the month following salary payment
LHDN CP38 guide: https://www.hasil.gov.my â€” search for 'CP38 Tax Deduction Order'; e-PCB Plus file format spec available via e-PCB Plus portal documentation
dependencies: Generate CP39 CSV with Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns
CP38 Additional Deduction Fields on Salary Slip

userStories.title: Self-Billed e-Invoice for Employee Expense Claims and Perquisite Reimbursements (MyInvois Phase 4)
userStories.description: While regular salary and payroll are exempt from Malaysia's e-invoicing mandate, employee expense claim reimbursements, medical claims, travel allowances paid as reimbursements, and employer-provided perquisite benefits are not exempt. Effective 1 January 2026 (Phase 4: RM1Mâ€“RM5M turnover), employers must issue self-billed e-invoices via MyInvois when reimbursing employees for business expenses where a third-party invoice exists. The payroll system's Expense Claim module must generate a self-billed e-invoice XML and submit it to the MyInvois API when an Expense Claim is approved and paid.
acceptanceCriteria: When an Expense Claim is approved and the submitting Company's custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase is Phase 4 or earlier, a 'Submit Self-Billed e-Invoice' button appears on the Expense Claim form
Clicking the button generates a LHDN-compliant UBL XML self-billed e-invoice with: seller = employee, buyer = company, line items from Expense Claim items, and submits to the MyInvois API
The Expense Claim stores the returned LHDN submission UUID, document number, and validation status in custom_myinvois_uuid, custom_myinvois_doc_no, and custom_myinvois_status fields

A compliance report 'Unpaired Expense Claims (e-Invoice Missing)' lists Expense Claims above RM50 that are approved-and-paid but have no MyInvois UUID, filtered by company mandate phase and claim date
Regular salary slip payments (BASIC, EPF, SOCSO, PCB deductions) do not trigger an e-invoice â€” only Expense Claim reimbursements do
technicalNotes: LHDN e-invoice exemption list: employment income (salary, wages, bonuses) is exempt; expense claim reimbursements are NOT exempt when a supplier e-invoice exists
Self-billed e-invoice: company issues the invoice on behalf of the employee-supplier (employee is the 'seller' in the UBL XML)
Phase 4 effective: 1 January 2026 for companies with RM1Mâ€“RM5M annual turnover
Use existing myinvois_erpgulf API integration (createxml module) for the UBL XML generation; map Expense Claim fields to UBL line items
dependencies: MyInvois Phase 4 (January 2026) â€” Set Mandatory e-Invoice Flag for Companies with RM1Mâ€“RM5M Annual Revenue

userStories.title: SOCSO Foreign Worker Invalidity Scheme â€” Enforce 0.5% Employee Deduction on Payslip from July 2024
userStories.description: Effective 1 July 2024, foreign workers who are under 55 when first contributing to PERKESO must be enrolled in the Invalidity Scheme in addition to the Employment Injury Scheme. This adds a 0.5% employee-share deduction from the foreign worker's salary (employer also pays 0.5% invalidity on top of the existing 1.25% employment injury). The payroll system must enforce this additional employee deduction for eligible foreign workers and reflect the new combined contribution rate (employer 1.75%, employee 0.5%) for all PERKESO contributions on the SOCSO summary report.
acceptanceCriteria: For employees where custom_is_foreign_worker = True and age_at_first_perkeso_contribution < 55, the SOCSO deduction engine applies the 0.5% Invalidity Scheme employee deduction in addition to the Employment Injury Scheme

Payslip shows two SOCSO deduction rows for eligible foreign workers: 'SOCSO Employment Injury (Employer)' (1.25% â€” employer borne) and 'SOCSO Invalidity Scheme (Employee)' (0.5% â€” deducted from salary)
The SOCSO contribution report separates Employment Injury and Invalidity Scheme contribution totals with effective date 2024-07-01 as the cutoff for foreign worker invalidity enrollment

Employees who were 55 or older at first PERKESO enrollment are flagged as 'Invalidity Scheme Not Applicable (age â‰¥55 at enrollment)' and only the Employment Injury rate (1.25% employer, 0% employee) is applied
Unit test: foreign worker enrolled at age 40 has both Employment Injury and Invalidity deductions; foreign worker enrolled at age 56 has only Employment Injury deduction
technicalNotes: PERKESO gazette: Employees' Social Security (Amendment of First Schedule) Order 2024 â€” effective 1 July 2024
New total SOCSO rates for foreign workers (age <55 at enrollment): employer 1.75% (1.25% EI + 0.5% IS), employee 0.5% (IS only)
Foreign workers age â‰¥55 at first enrollment: employer 1.25% (EI only), employee 0% â€” they are in the Employment Injury Scheme but not Invalidity
Payment to PERKESO: via the combined SOCSO+EIS txt file on ASSIST portal â€” the Invalidity Scheme contribution appears as a separate row type in the file
dependencies: SOCSO Foreign Worker Invalidity Scheme Inclusion Flag and Rate Update (July 2024)

userStories.title: Minimum Wage Enforcement â€” Penalty Quantum Awareness Panel and Ongoing Violation Daily Fine Tracker
userStories.description: Effective 1 August 2025, the RM1,700 minimum wage applies to all employers including those with fewer than 5 employees. Non-compliance carries fines of up to RM10,000 per employee for a first offence, up to RM1,000 per day for continuing offences after conviction, and up to RM20,000 or 5 years imprisonment for repeat offences. The payroll system's minimum wage validation already blocks non-compliant salary slips, but there is no dashboard showing the penalty exposure if violations are found in an audit (e.g., total underpaid months Ã— RM10,000 per employee).
acceptanceCriteria: The Minimum Wage Compliance Report gains a 'Penalty Exposure Estimate' column: for each employee-month where salary < RM1,700 is detected, the system calculates a worst-case penalty of RM10,000 Ã— number_of_non_compliant_employees and displays the aggregate total

A payroll dashboard widget 'Minimum Wage Compliance' shows: total employees checked this month, count of compliant employees, count of non-compliant employees, and the aggregate penalty exposure estimate
The widget displays three penalty tier descriptions: First offence: RM10,000 per employee; Continuing offence: RM1,000/day post-conviction; Repeat offence: RM20,000 or 5 years imprisonment
An audit history panel shows all payroll periods where minimum wage violations were detected or overridden, with the HR user who approved the override and the stated reason
The system confirms that domestic servants (with custom_is_domestic_servant = True) and apprentices (with custom_is_apprentice = True) are excluded from the minimum wage check and penalty calculation
technicalNotes: Minimum Wages Order 2024 enforcement from 1 August 2025 for all employers (Gazette P.U.(A) 479/2024)
Penalty provisions: National Wages Consultative Council Act 2011 (Act 732), S.36 â€” fine up to RM10,000 per employee per offence; S.37 â€” continuing offence up to RM1,000/day
Domestic servants, certain contract apprentices, and commission-only workers (where gross wage including commission â‰¥ RM1,700) may have special treatment
Enforcement authority: Department of Labour (JTK) â€” inspections and back-pay recovery orders are common enforcement actions
dependencies: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025
Domestic Servant Minimum Wage Exclusion Flag and Alternative Remuneration Tracker

userStories.title: Domestic Servant Minimum Wage Exclusion Flag and Alternative Remuneration Tracker
userStories.description: The Minimum Wages Order 2024 explicitly excludes domestic servants (maids, gardeners, personal drivers employed in a household) from the RM1,700 minimum wage requirement. The payroll system must allow HR to designate employees as domestic servants so the minimum wage validation does not incorrectly flag them. Labour rights advocacy and government reviews are ongoing for a separate domestic servant wage order; the system should record the agreed contractual wage for future comparison when a dedicated order is gazetted.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType gains a field custom_is_domestic_servant (Checkbox, default unchecked) with a tooltip: 'Tick if employee is a domestic servant as defined under EA 1955 S.2. Minimum Wages Order 2024 does not apply.'
When custom_is_domestic_servant = 1, the minimum wage validation (RM1,700 enforcement) is suppressed for that employee and no compliance alert is generated

A payroll report 'Domestic Servant Wage Register' lists all employees with custom_is_domestic_servant = 1, their agreed contractual wage, and a column 'Domestic Wage Order Applicable' (currently 'None â€” Pending Gazette')
When a Domestic Servant Minimum Wage Order is gazetted, the system settings allow HR to enter the new prescribed rate and the validation resumes for domestic servants at the new rate
An audit log records when the custom_is_domestic_servant flag is toggled, capturing who changed it and the date
technicalNotes: Minimum Wages Order 2024 (P.U.(A) 479/2024): Section 3 explicitly excludes domestic servants as defined in EA 1955 and Labour Ordinances (Sabah/Sarawak)
Domestic servant definition (EA S.2): person employed in connection with domestic work in a private dwelling house and who resides in that dwelling house
Government indicated a separate review for domestic servant wages â€” system should support future gazette activation without a code change
SOCSO ASSIST portal: domestic worker SOCSO contributions are mandatory since February 2021 â€” this flag must not affect SOCSO contribution calculation
dependencies: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025

userStories.title: HRD Corp SBL-KHAS â€” Direct Payment to Training Provider and Levy Utilisation Ledger
userStories.description: Under HRD Corp's SBL-Khas (Special Training Assistance Scheme), HRD Corp pays the approved training provider directly from the employer's accumulated levy â€” the employer does not pay upfront. The payroll system tracks levy contributions but does not record approved SBL-Khas grants, resulting in an inflated 'available levy balance' display that does not reflect committed training expenditures. A training grant ledger is needed to show the real available balance: total levy paid minus claimed or committed grants.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'HRD Corp Training Grant' DocType records: grant_type (SBL / SBL-Khas / Industrial), approval_date, training_start_date, training_end_date, training_provider, approved_amount, payment_status (Pending HRD Payment / Paid / Rejected)
HRDF Levy Settings displays a computed field custom_available_levy_balance = total_levy_paid_ytd - sum(approved_training_grants_ytd_unpaid)
When payment_status = 'Paid', the approved_amount is deducted from the total available balance; when 'Rejected', the amount is reinstated
A report 'HRD Corp Levy Utilisation Ledger' shows month-by-month: levy contributed, grants approved, grants paid, grants rejected, and running available balance
An alert fires if custom_available_levy_balance falls below RM500, warning HR that the levy balance may be insufficient for pending training claims
technicalNotes: SBL-Khas: HRD Corp pays training provider directly â€” no reimbursement to employer; employer's levy balance is debited when HRD Corp issues payment
HRD Corp levy rates: 1% of monthly salary for employers with 10+ employees; 0.5% voluntary for 5â€“9 employee SMEs
Grant submission via eTRiS portal at hrdcorp.gov.my â€” claims must be submitted within 6 months of training completion
Forfeiture rule: no claim for 2 years = levy forfeited (up to RM10,000 retained by HRD Corp) â€” related to the 'HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert' story
dependencies: HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier
HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert

userStories.title: Flexible Work Arrangement (FWA) Payroll Impact Tracker â€” Adjusted PCB Basis and Payslip Annotation
userStories.description: Under Employment Act S.60P (effective January 2023), employees on approved FWA arrangements may vary their hours, days, or place of work. Where an FWA results in reduced contractual hours or a shift from full-time to part-time (e.g., a 3-day work week), the ordinary rate of pay (ORP), overtime thresholds, and minimum wage compliance calculation must be prorated accordingly. The payroll system approves FWA requests (per the existing S.60P tracker) but does not automatically adjust the ORP divisor, overtime caps, or minimum wage check when an FWA changes the employee's effective working pattern.
acceptanceCriteria: The FWA Record DocType (from existing S.60P story) gains fields: custom_fwa_hours_per_week (Float) and custom_fwa_days_per_week (Integer) to capture the approved reduced work pattern
When an active FWA record exists for an employee, the payroll engine uses custom_fwa_hours_per_week to compute the prorated ORP divisor and overtime threshold for that payroll period

Minimum wage compliance check for FWA employees uses the prorated hourly rate: RM1,700 Ã· 26 Ã· normal daily hours Ã— fwa_daily_hours â€” a warning fires if the prorated rate falls below RM10.42/hour (RM1,700 Ã· 26 Ã· 8 Ã— 1.6 rounded)
Payslips for employees on FWA include an annotation: 'FWA approved from [start_date] to [end_date]: [N] days/[H] hours per week'
Unit test: full-time employee (5 days, 8h/day) converted to FWA (3 days, 8h/day) has ORP computed on 15.6 days per month (3/5 Ã— 26), not 26
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.60P: employee may apply to vary hours/days/place of work; employer must respond within 60 days â€” FWA request tracker already built
Prorated ORP for part-time FWA: follow Employment Act S.60I (ORP = monthly salary Ã· 26) but adjust salary to the prorated contracted amount
Minimum hourly rate: RM1,700 minimum wage Ã· 26 working days Ã· 8 hours = RM8.17/hour; FWA employees must still receive at least this per hour worked
PCB for FWA employees: PCB is calculated on actual gross remuneration â€” no special formula, but ORP-based allowances and rest day pay must reflect the FWA hours
dependencies: Track Flexible Work Arrangement Requests and 60-Day Employer Response

userStories.title: Budget 2026 â€” Civil Servant Voluntary EPF Contribution Tax Relief on TP1
userStories.description: Budget 2026 proposes extending the RM3,000 tax relief for voluntary EPF contributions to civil servants who are in pensionable positions (and thus do not make mandatory EPF contributions). Previously this relief was only available to private sector employees making voluntary top-up contributions. Civil servants who voluntarily contribute to EPF on top of their pension deductions can now claim this relief on their TP1, reducing their monthly PCB. The payroll system must add a civil servant voluntary EPF relief field to TP1 and adjust PCB computation accordingly.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration DocType adds custom_voluntary_epf_civil_servant (Currency, max RM3,000) visible only when employee custom_employment_sector = 'Government / Pensionable'
PCB calculator applies the RM3,000 deduction for civil servant voluntary EPF relief, combined within the existing RM4,000 EPF+life insurance combined relief cap
Employee DocType gains custom_employment_sector (Select: Private / Government / Pensionable / Statutory Body / Other) to distinguish civil servants from private sector employees
A validation warning prevents a private sector employee from claiming the civil servant voluntary EPF relief, with a message explaining it only applies to pensionable government employees
Unit test: civil servant employee claiming RM3,000 voluntary EPF + RM1,500 life insurance = RM4,500 total; system caps EPF+life insurance at RM4,000 and reduces life insurance to RM1,000
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 source: KPMG Malaysia flash alert â€” 'Budget 2026: Key Highlights Affecting Individuals' at kpmg.com/xx/en/our-insights/gms-flash-alert/flash-alert-2025-207.html
Pensionable civil servants make pension deductions (not EPF) â€” they can optionally open an EPF account for voluntary contributions
RM3,000 voluntary EPF relief sits within the combined RM4,000 cap (EPF + life insurance/takaful) under ITA Schedule 9
This is separate from the existing 'EPF i-Topup (Voluntary Excess Contribution)' story which covers private sector voluntary top-ups
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: Employee Share Option (ESOS) Year-of-Award PCB Assessment and Year-of-Exercise Payslip Annotation
userStories.description: LHDN treats gains from employee share option schemes (ESOS) as employment perquisites under ITA S.4(b), taxable in the year of award â€” not the year of exercise. This creates a mismatch: the employee exercises the option and realises cash in year N, but the tax was assessable in year N-X (when the option was awarded). The payroll system must capture the award date, award price, and market value at award for each ESOS grant, compute the perquisite value for the year of award, and include this in the EA Form for the correct year. The exercise event generates only a payslip annotation with no additional PCB impact.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Share Option Grant' DocType records: employee, grant_date, number_of_options, option_price, market_value_at_grant, vesting_schedule, exercise_date (nullable), exercise_market_value (nullable)

At grant date, the system computes perquisite_value = (market_value_at_grant - option_price) Ã— number_of_options and creates a draft Salary Component entry for inclusion in the EA Form for the grant year
The EA Form for the award year includes the ESOS perquisite value under the correct income section (perquisites) even if the employee has not yet exercised the option

When an exercise event is recorded, the Salary Slip for that month shows an informational annotation: 'ESOS exercised: [N] shares at RM[X]; PCB assessed in year of award [YYYY] â€” no additional PCB this month'
A report 'ESOS Grant Register' lists all active grants per employee with: grant date, award year, perquisite value assessed, exercise status, and EA Form inclusion status
technicalNotes: ITA S.4(b) perquisite treatment: ESOS gain = lower of (market value on exercise date - option price) or (market value on first exercisable date - option price); assessable in year of award
Source: Azmi & Associates legal guide at azmilaw.com on ESOS tax treatment in Malaysia
LHDN Public Ruling No. 11/2012 (Employee Share Scheme) â€” verify if superseded by a more recent ruling for post-2024 grants
This story handles the PCB/EA Form implications only; the corporate tax deduction for ESOS costs is a separate topic outside payroll scope
dependencies: ESOS/Share Option Gain Calculation

userStories.title: PCB 2026 Spec â€” Up-Skilling and Self-Enhancement Course Fee Relief Extension to YA2026
userStories.description: Budget 2026 extends the RM2,000 individual tax relief for up-skilling and self-enhancement course fees (claimable by employees) from YA2024 through YA2026. This relief covers HRDC-approved courses and other accredited training. The TP1 Declaration DocType must accept this relief for payroll years 2025 and 2026, and the PCB calculator must correctly exclude it for years where the relief has not been legislated (post-YA2026 unless further extended). A relief validity window must be enforced so the calculator does not incorrectly apply a time-limited relief in a future year.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration DocType field custom_upskilling_relief (Currency, max RM2,000) is available for tax years YA2024, YA2025, and YA2026 only; for YA2027 and beyond, the field is hidden with a tooltip: 'Up-skilling relief expired at YA2026 â€” check for gazette extension'
PCB calculator applies the RM2,000 up-skilling deduction only when the payroll year is 2024, 2025, or 2026; a warning appears if an employee has declared the relief for an out-of-scope year

LHDN Payroll Settings DocType has a 'TP1 Relief Validity' child table where each relief type stores: relief_name, max_amount, valid_from_ya, valid_to_ya â€” up-skilling is seeded as valid YA2024â€“YA2026
When the valid_to_ya is reached and no extension is detected, the system sends an annual reminder to LHDN Payroll Admin to verify and update the relief table
Unit test: employee declares RM2,000 up-skilling relief in YA2025 â†’ deduction applied; same declaration in YA2027 â†’ blocked with warning
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 source: LHDN PCB 2026 Amendment Spec and Ramco Malaysia Budget 2026 payroll summary at ramco.com
Up-skilling relief scope: courses recognized by Department of Skills Development (DSD) or approved by HRD Corp; maximum RM2,000 per assessment year
This relief is separate from the RM1,000 lifestyle relief â€” it has its own RM2,000 cap
Gazette reference: confirm via LHDN hasil.gov.my/en/legislation â€” search 'Income Tax (Relief for Up-Skilling Course) Order'
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration
Generate LHDN PCB 2026 Specification Annual Update Checklist and Version Flag

userStories.title: EIS Amendment 2025 â€” Enhanced Job Search Allowance and Training Benefits Display on Termination
userStories.description: The Employment Insurance System (Amendment) Bill 2025, tabled in Parliament 4 November 2025, proposes increasing the training fee ceiling from RM4,000 to RM7,000, the daily training allowance to RM30, and the early re-employment allowance from 25% to 50% of unpaid Job Search Allowance. When processing employee termination or redundancy in the payroll system, the EIS benefit entitlement panel must display these updated proposed rates clearly, helping HR teams communicate accurately with retrenched employees about their EIS benefits while noting the pending gazette status.
acceptanceCriteria: The Employee Termination form includes an 'EIS Benefit Entitlement Summary' panel that shows: Job Search Allowance rate table (% of last salary by months of contribution), Training Fee Allowance (RM7,000 max â€” proposed), Daily Training Allowance (RM30 â€” proposed), Early Re-employment Allowance (50% of remaining JSA â€” proposed)
The panel displays a banner: 'Rates marked as proposed are based on EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 (tabled 4 Nov 2025). Confirm gazette date before quoting to employees.'
EIS benefit rates are stored in a configurable 'EIS Benefit Rate Settings' DocType (not hardcoded) with a gazette_status field (Proposed / Gazetted / Effective) per rate
When gazette_status = 'Gazetted', the 'proposed' banner is automatically replaced with an 'Effective from [date]' label
A link to the PERKESO ASSIST portal EIS claim page is displayed on the termination form for the employee's self-service claim submission
technicalNotes: EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025: Dewan Rakyat 4 November 2025; as of research date (March 2026) gazette status to be confirmed
Current (pre-amendment) EIS training fee max: RM4,000; proposed: RM7,000
Early re-employment allowance: currently 25% of unpaid JSA; proposed 50%
FMM (Federation of Malaysian Manufacturers) supports the Bill: https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2025/11/06/fmm-supports-enhancement-of-eis-benefits
dependencies: EIS Contribution Ceiling RM6,000

userStories.title: e-PCB Plus Transition â€” Archive and Decommission Legacy e-Data PCB and eCP39 File Submission Workflows
userStories.description: The LHDN e-PCB Plus system (fully operational from 3 February 2025) replaces the legacy e-PCB, e-Data PCB, and eCP39 portals. Employers who continue to use legacy file formats or submission paths will encounter upload rejections. The payroll system must decommission legacy PCB submission options, migrate any historic submission records to the new e-PCB Plus status tracking model, and display a clear migration guide for HR teams still using the old workflows. Legacy submission records must be retained for audit purposes but clearly labelled as historical.
acceptanceCriteria: Legacy PCB submission options ('Submit via e-Data PCB', 'Submit via eCP39') are hidden from the UI and replaced by a single 'Submit via e-PCB Plus (MyTax)' workflow with a link to the MyTax portal
Any existing submission records created under the legacy e-Data PCB or eCP39 workflow are migrated to a 'Legacy (Pre-Feb 2025)' status category and retained in the audit history

A migration banner is displayed on the LHDN Payroll module home page until dismissed by HR Admin: 'e-PCB Plus is now mandatory from February 2025. Legacy e-Data PCB / eCP39 submissions are no longer accepted by LHDN.'
The Statutory Compliance Calendar replaces 'e-Data PCB submission' and 'eCP39 submission' events with a single 'e-PCB Plus Monthly Submission (due 15th)' recurring event
Unit test: attempting to create a new submission record with status_type = 'e-Data PCB' raises a validation error redirecting the user to the e-PCB Plus workflow
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus launch: 24 September 2024 (pilot); mandatory from 3 February 2025 at MyTax portal
LHDN directive: all employers using e-PCB, e-Data PCB, and eCP39 must migrate to e-PCB Plus by 15 January 2025 for December 2024 submissions
Legacy portals are being phased out â€” employers uploading to the old portals after the cutoff receive error: 'Service discontinued'
Source: The Rakyat Post (8 Jan 2025), Pandahrms e-PCB Plus guide, and LHDN official announcement at hasil.gov.my
dependencies: Generate CP39 CSV with Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns
e-PCB Plus Successful Submission â€” Store Acknowledgement Reference

userStories.title: Employment Pass Succession Plan Compliance Tracker for EP Categories II and III
userStories.description: Effective 1 June 2026, all new and renewal Employment Pass applications for Category II (RM10,000â€“RM19,999/month, max 10 years) and Category III (RM5,000â€“RM9,999/month, max 5 years) must include a structured succession plan demonstrating how the expatriate's role will be transferred to a local employee within the approved tenure. Immigration will reject renewal applications without an approved succession plan. Employers with multiple EP holders in these categories face a significant compliance gap if no structured tracking exists in their HR system to manage plan submission status, knowledge transfer milestones, and local successor assignments.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType gains a custom_ep_succession_plan_status Select field ('Not Required' / 'Draft' / 'Submitted to IMI' / 'Approved' / 'Overdue') visible only when visa_type is 'Employment Pass' and ep_category in ('Category II', 'Category III')

Succession plan fields capture: local_successor_employee (Link to Employee), knowledge_transfer_start_date, knowledge_transfer_target_date, succession_plan_document (Attach), ep_succession_plan_submission_date, ep_succession_plan_imi_reference
An automated alert fires to the HR Manager 90 days before EP renewal date if custom_ep_succession_plan_status is not 'Approved'
A Succession Plan Compliance Report lists all Cat II/III EP holders with their plan status, local successor, and knowledge transfer timeline completion percentage
On EP renewal date with status not 'Approved', the system creates a Compliance Alert DocType record with severity 'Critical' and routes it to the Employer Representative
technicalNotes: Revised EP policy effective 1 June 2026 â€” Cabinet approval 17 October 2025; announced via IMI portal
EP Category II: RM10,000â€“RM19,999/month, max 10 years; Category III: RM5,000â€“RM9,999/month, max 5 years
Succession plan must include: local successor ID, role transfer breakdown, monthly training schedule (24â€“36 months), assessment milestones, contingency protocols, executive sign-off
Link to existing Compliance Alert or custom lhdn_payroll_integration compliance framework
IMI official announcement: https://esd.imi.gov.my/portal/latest-news/announcement/announcement-266-ep-salary-policy-2026/
dependencies: Employment Pass Minimum Salary Threshold Validator (June 2026 Revised EP Policy)

userStories.title: Employment Pass Maximum Tenure Expiry Alert (Category III: 5 Years, Category II: 10 Years)
