userStories.description: Budget 2026 amended TP1 to extend child care centre relief eligibility to children up to 12 years old (previously capped at 6 years), covering registered daily care and after-school transit centres. The existing story 'Budget 2026 TP1: Permanent RM3,000 Childcare Relief for JKM-Registered Transit Centres' focuses on JKM-registered centre classification, but does not update the core age eligibility validation rule from â‰¤6 to â‰¤12 in the PCB calculation engine. This is a distinct validator change in the MTD calculator.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 child care relief validator accepts child ages 1â€“12 years; children aged 7â€“12 previously rejected are now accepted
RM3,000 relief cap is maintained regardless of how many children in the 1â€“12 age range are declared
PCB recalculation triggers correctly when an employee adds a child aged 7â€“12 to their TP1 declaration
Existing children aged â‰¤6 on prior TP1 records are unaffected; new age upper bound of 12 applies from YA2026 onward
technicalNotes: Update age validation rule in TP1 child care section: child.age <= 12 (was child.age <= 6)
Applies to licensed/registered daily care centres and after-school transit centres; registration type validation unchanged
Source form: FORM PCB TP1 (1/2026); effective from YA2026
dependencies: Budget 2026 TP1: Permanent RM3,000 Childcare Relief for JKM-Registered Transit Centres

userStories.title: PCB MTD Minimum Deduction Guard: Zero Out Monthly Deduction When Computed PCB Amount Is Less Than RM10
userStories.description: LHDN guidelines specify that PCB/MTD amounts less than RM10 per month must not be deducted from employees' wages. The PCB calculator must include a minimum-threshold guard as a final post-computation step: if the computed monthly PCB falls between RM0.01 and RM9.99, the deduction for that month must be set to RM0. This edge case is not explicitly addressed in any existing PCB calculation story and can cause payroll discrepancies and LHDN reconciliation issues for low-income employees near the PCB threshold.
acceptanceCriteria: After applying the full MTD formula (including all reliefs and rebates), if the resulting monthly PCB amount is between RM0.01 and RM9.99 inclusive, the system sets the salary slip PCB deduction to RM0
The system does not carry forward sub-RM10 amounts to subsequent months; each month's calculation is independent
Salary slip clearly shows RM0 PCB deduction with a tooltip/note: 'PCB below RM10 monthly threshold â€” not deducted per LHDN guideline'
PCB calculator test suite includes cases where computed PCB rounds to RM8.50, RM9.99, RM10.00, and RM10.01 to verify correct handling at the boundary
technicalNotes: LHDN guideline: 'PCB amounts less than RM10 per month are not deducted' â€” applies after full MTD computation
Typically affects employees earning just above the PCB threshold (RM2,851 single / RM3,851 married) with significant reliefs
Threshold is RM10 per month, not annual â€” do not aggregate across months
Implementation: add a final check in the PCB calculation function: if pcb_amount < 10: return 0
dependencies: Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)

userStories.title: Employment Act S.26 Payment in Lieu of Notice: PCB-Taxable but EPF/SOCSO/EIS-Exempt Payroll Classification
userStories.description: Under Employment Act Section 26 and EPF/SOCSO legislation, Payment in Lieu of Notice (PILON) â€” compensation paid when an employer terminates without serving the contractual notice period â€” is treated as employment income subject to PCB, but is specifically excluded from EPF contributions (not 'wages' under EPF Act) and SOCSO/EIS contributions. Many payroll systems incorrectly calculate EPF and SOCSO on PILON amounts. A dedicated PILON salary component type with correct statutory flags is required, distinct from contractual severance gratuity.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Payment in Lieu of Notice (PILON)' salary component type exists with is_pcb_component=True, is_epf_applicable=False, is_socso_applicable=False, is_eis_applicable=False
When PILON is included in a final salary slip, PCB is calculated on the PILON amount as employment income, but EPF/SOCSO/EIS contributions are not triggered
PILON amount appears in EA Form Part B as additional taxable employment income for the year
System prevents PILON from being auto-classified as contractual severance gratuity (which may qualify for ITA Schedule 4B tax exemption)
Test case: employee with monthly salary RM5,000 receives 2-month PILON (RM10,000); verify PCB computed on RM10,000 and EPF/SOCSO/EIS at RM0
technicalNotes: EPF Act 1991 definition of 'wages' in Schedule 3 excludes gratuities and payments in lieu of notice
SOCSO Act 1969 Section 2: 'wages' excludes PILON â€” no contribution required
PCB treatment: PILON is taxable employment income per ITA 1967 Section 13(1)(a)
Distinct from termination benefits/gratuity which may be exempt under ITA Schedule 4B
dependencies: Add bonus/irregular payment classification for PCB differentiation
Implement Termination and Lay-Off Benefits Calculator

userStories.title: Foreign-Sourced Income Remittance PCB Exemption Treatment for Malaysian Tax Residents (Effective to YA2026)
userStories.description: Under current exemption orders, certain foreign-sourced income received by Malaysian tax residents and remitted into Malaysia is exempt from income tax (investment income exempt until December 31, 2036; other categories until December 31, 2026). For expatriates and Malaysian employees with offshore income, employers need to distinguish foreign-sourced exempt remittances from Malaysian employment income when computing PCB, and correctly exclude qualifying amounts from the PCB taxable base. This is distinct from the existing DTA 183-day tracker which handles treaty-based exemptions for short-term visiting employees.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record allows flagging of 'Foreign-Sourced Income Exemption' with: source country, exemption basis (Remittance Exemption Order / DTA Article), exempt amount declared for the year, and expiry YA
PCB calculator excludes declared foreign-sourced exempt remittances from annual taxable income when computing monthly MTD for residents
System validates that exemption is only applied to tax residents (â‰¥182 days in Malaysia) and rejects for non-residents
HR records the exemption order reference number for LHDN audit trail
Warning fires if exemption is claimed for income types not covered by the remittance exemption orders (e.g., active business income from foreign operations)
technicalNotes: Exemption under ITA 1967 via P.U.(A) exemption orders: certain FSI remitted by residents exempt until Dec 31, 2026 (investment income until Dec 31, 2036)
Distinct from DTA-based exemption (existing story covers 183-day rule); this covers statutory FSI remittance exemption order
Not applicable to income from employment performed in Malaysia â€” only to offshore/investment income remitted to Malaysia
PWC Malaysia tax summary 2025 confirms transitional FSI exemption rules in force
dependencies: Implement DTA 183-Day Residency Tracker and Short-Term Assignee PCB Exemption Workflow
Implement Expatriate Gross-Up Calculator and DTA Country Table

userStories.title: PERKESO LINDUNG 24/7 â€” Implement Employee Second-Category Contribution Deduction Engine and Gazette-Rate Activation Workflow
userStories.description: The Employees' Social Security (Amendment) Bill 2025 was passed by Parliament on 2 December 2025, introducing the LINDUNG 24/7 Non-Occupational Accident Scheme. A fundamental structural change is that BOTH employers AND employees must now contribute under Second Category â€” previously only employers contributed to Second Category (Invalidity Scheme). Contribution rates and effective date are pending PERKESO gazette announcement expected Q1/Q2 2026. All payroll systems must add the new employee-side Second Category deduction and update PERKESO ASSIST submission data once rates are officially gazetted.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'LINDUNG 24/7 Employee Contribution Rate' configuration field on PERKESO/SOCSO Settings with a 'Pending Gazette' status flag and activation date
When rate is entered and activation date reached, payroll engine automatically computes and deducts employee LINDUNG 24/7 contribution from gross pay per the gazette rate
Payslip displays new line item 'LINDUNG 24/7 (Employee)' as a separate deduction, distinct from existing First Category employee contribution
Employer LINDUNG 24/7 contribution amount added to employer cost calculation
PERKESO ASSIST e-Caruman submission updated to include new LINDUNG 24/7 contribution amounts for both employer and employee per employee
System alert sent to HR Manager when gazette rate is entered, prompting payroll recalculation from the gazette effective date
Pre-gazette payroll runs display a compliance warning: 'LINDUNG 24/7 rates not yet gazetted â€” deduction not applied'
technicalNotes: Parliament passed amendment on 2 December 2025; contribution rates TBD via separate gazette announcement

Currently Second Category is employer-only (Invalidity Scheme); new LINDUNG 24/7 requires both employer+employee contribution â€” materially different from the label-only rebranding covered in existing LINDUNG story
PERKESO ASSIST e-Caruman CSV/API may need new contribution code/column for LINDUNG 24/7 scheme â€” monitor perkeso.gov.my/en/rate-of-contribution.html
Expected implementation Q1 or at latest Q2 2026 per PERKESO announcement
Related: existing 'PERKESO LINDUNG Scheme Rebranding' story covers UI labels only; this story covers actual contribution computation and payslip deduction

userStories.title: PERKESO LINDUNG 24/7 â€” Update e-Caruman Submission File with New Third-Scheme Contribution Code
userStories.description: The PERKESO LINDUNG 24/7 amendment introduces a new protection scheme distinct from First Category (Employment Injury) and Second Category (Invalidity). When rates are gazetted, the PERKESO ASSIST e-Caruman electronic submission file will require a new contribution code for LINDUNG 24/7 amounts from both employer and employee. The existing e-Caruman generator handles only First and Second Category codes and must be extended for the new scheme without breaking existing submissions.
acceptanceCriteria: e-Caruman CSV/upload generator updated to include new LINDUNG 24/7 contribution code column for both employer and employee portions
Pre-gazette: LINDUNG 24/7 column exists but outputs zero / is flagged 'Pending Gazette' â€” file still passes PERKESO ASSIST validation
Post-gazette activation: LINDUNG 24/7 employer and employee contribution amounts correctly populated and accepted by PERKESO ASSIST upload
Historical e-Caruman files for periods before gazette effective date do not include LINDUNG 24/7 values, maintaining backward compatibility
Contribution receipt from PERKESO displays LINDUNG 24/7 amounts as a separate line in the system audit record
PERKESO ASSIST API upload response parsed to confirm new scheme fields accepted without rejection error
technicalNotes: Monitor PERKESO ASSIST developer portal for updated file format specification once LINDUNG 24/7 is gazetted
May require new column in e-Caruman CSV (e.g., Third_Category_Employer, Third_Category_Employee) or new XML element
Existing e-Caruman generation code lives in lhdn_payroll_integration/lhdn_payroll_integration/services/ â€” extend without breaking current First/Second Category columns
Rate announcement expected Q1/Q2 2026 at https://perkeso.gov.my/en/our-services/employer-employee/kadar-caruman.html
dependencies: PERKESO LINDUNG 24/7 â€” Implement Employee Second-Category Contribution Deduction Engine and Gazette-Rate Activation Workflow
Generate SOCSO and EIS e-Caruman Upload File (PERKESO ASSIST Portal)

userStories.title: MyInvois RM10,000 Non-Consolidatable Transaction Rule â€” Route Large Payroll Expense Reimbursements to Individual Self-Billed e-Invoice
userStories.description: Effective 1 January 2026, LHDN MyInvois rules prohibit consolidating any single transaction exceeding RM10,000 into a consolidated e-invoice. Payroll self-billed e-invoices for large employee expense reimbursements, relocation allowances, one-off perquisites, or BIK items exceeding RM10,000 per transaction must be issued as individual (non-consolidated) self-billed e-invoices with real-time MyInvois validation. The existing self-billed expense claim story does not address this amount-based routing split between consolidated and individual e-invoice workflows.
acceptanceCriteria: On Expense Claim or payroll perquisite generation, system checks if the individual transaction amount exceeds RM10,000
Transactions > RM10,000 are routed exclusively to individual self-billed e-invoice workflow, excluded from consolidated monthly batch
Transactions â‰¤ RM10,000 remain eligible for monthly consolidated self-billed e-invoice as before
HR/payroll user receives a clear warning when a claim exceeds the RM10,000 threshold before batch consolidation is executed
Audit log records the routing decision (individual vs consolidated) and reason (RM10,000 amount threshold) for each payroll expense transaction
Individual self-billed e-invoices for large amounts submitted to MyInvois API with all required invoice-level fields and receive an individual UUID from MyInvois
technicalNotes: RM10,000 threshold applies per single transaction, not per aggregate employee per month
Most affected payroll items: large travel reimbursements, relocation packages, one-off medical claims, ex-gratia payments classified as perquisites
The consolidated e-invoice limit for sub-RM10,000 items is unaffected â€” only the routing logic changes above the threshold
LHDN clarified effective date is 1 January 2026; Phase 4 companies have a grace period until 31 December 2026 for penalties
Reference: MyInvois SDK v2.1 for individual invoice vs consolidated invoice schema differences
dependencies: Self-Billed e-Invoice for Employee Expense Claims and Perquisite Reimbursements (MyInvois Phase 4)

userStories.title: Gig Workers Act 872 â€” Enforce PERKESO Deduction on Separate Ministerial Gazette Enforcement Date, Not Royal Assent
userStories.description: The Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872) received Royal Assent on 16 December 2025 and was gazetted on 31 December 2025. However, the Act explicitly states enforcement commences on a date separately determined by the Minister via gazette notification. As of March 2026, stakeholder engagement is still intensifying and enforcement has not started. The existing PRD story activates PERKESO deductions 'on Royal Assent', which is technically premature. A configurable enforcement-date gate must control when the 1.25% PERKESO deduction begins, preventing incorrect premature deductions from gig worker earnings.
acceptanceCriteria: Company-level configuration field 'Gig Workers Act 872 Enforcement Date' â€” initially empty/disabled
PERKESO LINDUNG KENDIRI deductions (1.25% per transaction) are NOT triggered until the enforcement date field is populated and the date is reached
System status panel shows: 'Act 872: Gazetted 31 Dec 2025 | Enforcement Date: PENDING MINISTERIAL GAZETTE'
When HR Admin enters the official ministerial enforcement date, system schedules deduction activation from that date
Alert sent to HR Admin 30 days and 7 days before enforcement date once entered
All platform-provider gig worker payment processing displays 'Pre-Enforcement â€” No PERKESO Deduction' until activation
If enforcement date is entered retroactively, system flags affected prior payroll periods for retroactive review
technicalNotes: Act 872 Section 1(2): 'This Act comes into operation on a date to be appointed by the Minister by notification in the Gazette'

Existing story 'Gig Workers Act 2025 â€” Platform Provider Registration as PERKESO Contributing Entity with Mandatory 1.25% LINDUNG KENDIRI Deduction on Royal Assent' has incorrect activation trigger â€” this gate must be applied
SOCSO target contribution rate: 1.25% of earnings per ride/delivery transaction
Monitor kesuma.gov.my and perkeso.gov.my for ministerial enforcement gazette notification
As of March 2026 gig worker talks are intensifying â€” enforcement date still unannounced per theedgemalaysia.com
dependencies: Gig Workers Act 2025 â€” Platform Provider Registration as PERKESO Contributing Entity with Mandatory 1.25% LINDUNG KENDIRI Deduction on Royal Assent

userStories.title: Malaysia Minimum Wage NWCC Biannual Review â€” Configurable Rate Registry with Gazette History and Bulk Below-Minimum Alert
userStories.description: The National Wages Consultative Council (NWCC) reviews minimum wage every two years. The current RM1,700 rate (universal from August 2025) will be reviewed again in 2026/2027. The system currently treats RM1,700 as a hardcoded constant. A configurable minimum wage rate parameter with gazette history, effective-date versioning, and a bulk below-minimum alert ensures the system adapts to future NWCC gazette announcements without code changes â€” preventing any pay period where employees are underpaid against the new minimum.
acceptanceCriteria: Minimum Wage configuration DocType with fields: Rate (RM/month), Effective Date, Gazette Number, Gazette Date, Status (Active / Superseded)
Rate change history log: all previous minimum wage rates and their effective dates preserved and non-deletable for audit
On minimum wage rate creation/update, system scans all active employee salary records and flags any basic salary below the new rate
Bulk action: 'Apply Minimum Wage Update' to set all flagged employees to the new minimum with confirmation and audit log
HR dashboard shows: current active rate, gazette date, and an 'NWCC Review Advisory' when within 6 months of the 2-year anniversary of the current rate
Payroll pre-validation check blocks salary slip submission if any employee's computed gross is below current minimum wage rate
Penalty calculator auto-updates fine quantum calculations when the rate is changed in the registry
technicalNotes: Current active rate: RM1,700/month, effective universally from 1 August 2025 (small employers), 1 February 2025 (5+ employees)
Previous rate: RM1,500 effective 1 May 2022
NWCC statutory review cycle: at minimum every 2 years under Minimum Wages Order framework â€” next review expected 2026/2027
Monitor mohr.gov.my gazette page for NWCC announcement
Existing stories 'Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers' cover enforcement rules; this story adds the configurable rate infrastructure layer
dependencies: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025
Minimum Wage Enforcement â€” Penalty Quantum Awareness Panel and Ongoing Violation Daily Fine Tracker

userStories.title: TP1(1/2026) Domestic Tourism and Cultural Attraction Relief â€” YA2026-Only Expiry Guard and Auto-Deactivation
userStories.description: Budget 2026 introduces a RM1,000 tax relief for entrance fees to domestic tourist attractions and cultural programs â€” explicitly for Year of Assessment 2026 only. Without a hard expiry guard, this temporary relief may be incorrectly applied in YA2027 payroll runs, causing over-deduction of PCB and potential LHDN audit exposure. A time-bounded eligibility flag with automatic deactivation from January 2027 prevents carry-forward errors and ensures PCB is compliant for both YA2026 and YA2027 simultaneously.
acceptanceCriteria: Domestic tourism/cultural attraction relief line item on TP1 form is active only for payroll PCB periods January 2026 â€“ December 2026
System automatically disables the relief input field from 1 January 2027 with expiry notice: 'Domestic Tourism Relief (YA2026 only) â€” Expired'
Existing employee TP1 declarations with this relief generate a HR alert when YA transitions to 2027: 'YA2026 temporary relief has expired â€” PCB will increase from January 2027'
EA Form template for YA2026 correctly includes the relief; YA2027 EA Form template excludes it
PCB recalculation for retroactive YA2026 adjustments correctly applies the time-bound eligibility; YA2027 recalculations ignore this relief item
Audit log shows 'Domestic Tourism Relief: Applied (YA2026 only)' or 'Not Applied (outside YA boundary)' per PCB computation
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 announcement specifies 'YA 2026' only â€” this is a time-limited relief, not permanent
Similar time-bound pattern to EV Charging relief (once per 2-YA biennial restriction) and PRS relief extension to YA2030
LHDN official TP1(1/2026) explanatory notes: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/i1lcd0wn/bi-explanatory-notes-for-from-tp1-2026.pdf
The existing story 'TP1(1/2026): Capture New Domestic Tourism Attraction Expenses Relief (RM1,000)' adds the field â€” this story adds the YA-expiry enforcement layer
dependencies: TP1(1/2026): Capture New Domestic Tourism Attraction Expenses Relief (RM1,000) in Employee PCB Declaration

userStories.title: EIS S.45F MYFutureJobs Job Vacancy Notification â€” Pre-Build Portal API Integration for Post-Moratorium Readiness
userStories.description: The EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 Section 45F requires employers to notify PERKESO via the MYFutureJobs portal when a vacancy is filled, within 7 days â€” penalty up to RM10,000 per violation. A 2-year moratorium is in effect from December 2025. The existing PRD story covers moratorium suppression only. This story builds the actual MYFutureJobs API submission workflow, notification reference number storage, and 7-day deadline tracker so the system is immediately compliant the moment the moratorium expires â€” with zero deployment lag.
acceptanceCriteria: Job Opening DocType gains new fields: 'S.45F MYFutureJobs Notification Status' (Draft/Suppressed-Moratorium/Submitted/Overdue) and 'PERKESO Notification Reference Number'
When a Job Opening is marked Filled (Employee record linked), system creates a pending S.45F notification task
During active moratorium: task status is set to 'Suppressed â€” Moratorium Active' and not submitted to MYFutureJobs
Moratorium end-date configuration field; once moratorium expires, newly filled jobs auto-trigger with 7-day submission deadline
Overdue alert fires if a filled vacancy exceeds 7 days without a PERKESO reference number after moratorium ends
HR compliance report shows all filled jobs in the past 30 days with moratorium/submission status, reference numbers, and overdue count
technicalNotes: MYFutureJobs portal: myfuturejobs.gov.my â€” verify if employer API or digital form submission is available for S.45F notifications
S.45F notification must be 'in writing' to SOCSO â€” may be satisfied by MYFutureJobs portal digital submission
Moratorium duration: up to 2 years from Bill passage (December 2025) â€” estimated moratorium end: December 2027
Fine: not exceeding RM10,000 per violation on conviction
SMEs were given extra time per PERKESO December 2025 announcement â€” moratorium phase is indefinitely in effect until gazette
dependencies: EIS Amendment 2025 S.45F â€” Moratorium Phase Suppression and Voluntary Job Vacancy Reporting Module

userStories.title: EPF Akaun Emas Routing for Employees Aged 55+ in i-Akaun Employer CSV Upload
userStories.description: When an employee reaches age 55, all existing EPF accounts are consolidated into Akaun 55, and any new contributions thereafter flow exclusively into Akaun Emas (locked until age 60) â€” NOT the standard 75/15/10 three-account split. The i-Akaun employer CSV upload file must correctly route post-55 contributions to Akaun Emas rather than applying the Akaun Persaraan/Sejahtera/Fleksibel split, otherwise contributions may be misallocated. The existing story 'Update EPF i-Akaun Employer Contribution CSV Upload Format for Three-Account (75/15/10) Allocation' only handles the under-55 case.
acceptanceCriteria: Employees aged 55 or above at payroll period end date must have EPF contributions flagged to route 100% to Akaun Emas in the generated i-Akaun CSV, not the 75/15/10 split
The i-Akaun CSV row for a 55+ employee uses the correct account type code or flag distinguishing Akaun Emas from the three-account structure
An employee who turns 55 during the payroll month must switch routing from that payroll period onwards
Payroll run preview displays 'Akaun Emas' as the EPF destination for all affected employees
Unit test covers: employee under 55 â†’ three-account split; employee exactly 55 â†’ Akaun Emas; employee 60+ â†’ exempt-or-voluntary path
technicalNotes: KWSP: 'Should you choose to continue working after the age of 55, all further contributions you make will be credited in your Akaun Emas, to be withdrawn only upon reaching age 60'
Confirm the exact EPF i-Akaun CSV column/flag for Akaun Emas from KWSP Easy Guides for Employers at kwsp.gov.my/en/others/resource-centre/easy-guides/employer
Age calculation must compare employee.date_of_birth + 55 years against payroll_period_end_date
The existing 'Age 60' story handles rate transitions (0% employee contribution after 60); this story handles CSV account routing for the 55-60 window
dependencies: Update EPF i-Akaun Employer Contribution CSV Upload Format for Three-Account (75/15/10) Allocation
Implement Age-Based EPF/SOCSO/EIS Statutory Rate Transitions at Age 60

userStories.title: Sabah/Sarawak Labour Ordinance 2025 (Acts A1753/A1754) â€” Extend Payroll Statutory Protections to All East Malaysia Employees Regardless of Salary
userStories.description: The Labour Ordinance of Sabah (Amendment) Act 2025 (Act A1753) and Labour Ordinance of Sarawak (Amendment) Act 2025 (Act A1754) came into force on 1 May 2025, removing the prior salary cap of RM2,500/month for Labour Ordinance coverage. All East Malaysia employees are now entitled to statutory protections (overtime, public holiday pay, termination benefits, late-payment remedies) regardless of salary. The existing story 'Add Sabah/Sarawak Labour Jurisdiction Flag on Employee' only creates the flag; this story wires the flag into actual payroll compliance enforcement so higher-paid East Malaysia employees (previously exempt) are now correctly covered.
acceptanceCriteria: All payroll compliance checks (overtime eligibility, public holiday pay, termination benefits) must apply to Sabah/Sarawak jurisdiction-flagged employees regardless of their monthly salary, effective 1 May 2025
The RM2,500 salary cap filter that previously excluded higher-paid East Malaysia employees from Labour Ordinance provisions must be removed from all calculation branches
Overtime, public holiday premium, and termination benefit calculators must activate for East Malaysia employees earning above RM2,500 who were previously exempted
Payroll compliance reports for East Malaysia employees must reference the applicable ordinance (Sabah Labour Ordinance or Sarawak Labour Ordinance) rather than Employment Act 1955
Transition logic: compliance calculations before 1 May 2025 for previously-exempt higher-salary East Malaysia employees retain old (exempt) behaviour; on or after 1 May 2025 the new rules apply
technicalNotes: Act A1753 (Sabah): Royal Assent 7 March 2025, gazetted 20 March 2025, effective 1 May 2025
Act A1754 (Sarawak): same effective date 1 May 2025
The amendments standardise East Malaysia rules with EA 1955 as amended in January 2023
Penalty for violations increased from RM10,000 to RM50,000 â€” the RM50,000 penalty alert story below covers this
dependencies: Add Sabah/Sarawak Labour Jurisdiction Flag on Employee

userStories.title: AIS e-CP8D vs EA Form 2026 Cross-Validation â€” Reconcile Income Totals, BIK, VOLA, and ESOS Before Either Document Is Submitted
userStories.description: LHDN requires employers to submit the e-CP8D (Annual Income Statement / AIS upload) to MyTax by 25 February 2026 and distribute EA Forms to employees by 28 February 2026. Both documents must report identical income components: gross remuneration, Benefits-in-Kind (BIK), Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA), ESOS gains, tax-exempt allowances, and PCB deducted. If the two documents diverge, LHDN can flag the employer for audit. No existing story cross-checks these two outputs against each other before either is submitted â€” they are generated independently with no reconciliation gate.
acceptanceCriteria: Before the e-CP8D upload is triggered, the system compares each employee's e-CP8D income totals against the corresponding EA Form sections (A, B, C, D) for the same assessment year
Discrepancies exceeding RM1 in any income category (gross pay, BIK, VOLA, ESOS, EPF, PCB, zakat) block submission and display a line-by-line reconciliation report identifying the source of divergence
The reconciliation report identifies which salary slips, expense claims, or manual entries caused the discrepancy
HR can approve a documented variance below an employer-configurable tolerance threshold, with the approval logged for audit
A reconciliation status (Matched / Variance-Approved / Blocked) is stored on both the EA Form and e-CP8D records for each employee-year
technicalNotes: e-CP8D data scope: gross remuneration, BIK, VOLA, ESOS/remuneration arrears, tax-exempt allowances, MTD/PCB, EPF, zakat â€” these mirror EA Form sections A/B/C/D
e-CP8D pre-fills employee e-Filing from 1 March 2026; incorrect data causes employee e-Filing rejections â€” employer liability is significant
This story is an orchestration/comparison layer on top of the existing EA Form and AIS generation modules
Leverage Python difflib or a field-by-field dict comparison on the generated data objects before API submission
dependencies: Rebuild EA Form to Include All Mandatory LHDN Section A/B/C/D Fields
LHDN AIS Employer Upload â€” Auto-Submit Employee Remuneration Data to MyTax by 25 February Deadline
Generate CP8D Annual Employee Remuneration Return

userStories.title: Sabah/Sarawak Labour Ordinance 2025 â€” Apply 45-Hour Weekly Working Hours Cap to East Malaysia Jurisdiction-Flagged Employees
userStories.description: Prior to 1 May 2025, the Sabah and Sarawak Labour Ordinances permitted up to 48 working hours per week. The 2025 amendments reduced this to 45 hours per week, aligned with Peninsular Malaysia's Employment Act. The existing story 'Implement Working Hours Compliance Check (45-Hour Weekly Limit)' likely targets EA 1955 employees only. For East Malaysia employees with the jurisdiction flag, the limit changed from 48 to 45 hours effective 1 May 2025, and overtime must now be triggered from 45 hours (not 48), impacting payroll for East Malaysia workers on 46-48 hour rosters.
acceptanceCriteria: Working hours compliance check applies a 45-hour weekly maximum to all employees flagged as Sabah or Sarawak jurisdiction, effective 1 May 2025
Hours between 45 and 48 per week for East Malaysia employees on or after 1 May 2025 must be classified as overtime and attract overtime premium pay
East Malaysia employee timesheets or rosters showing more than 45 hours/week from 1 May 2025 trigger a compliance warning
Pre-1 May 2025 records for East Malaysia employees honour the old 48-hour limit â€” no retroactive reclassification
The working hours compliance rule set branching on jurisdiction flag: Peninsular â†’ EA 1955 (45 hrs effective Jan 2023), Sabah/Sarawak â†’ 45 hrs effective 1 May 2025
technicalNotes: Baker McKenzie summary confirms working hours reduction to 45 hrs/week as part of the 2025 ordinance standardisation
The existing '45-Hour Weekly Limit' story may already use a global limit without jurisdiction distinction â€” this story adds the jurisdiction-specific effective date for East Malaysia
This story will increase East Malaysia payroll cost for employers who previously rostered 46-48 hours without overtime
dependencies: Add Sabah/Sarawak Labour Jurisdiction Flag on Employee
Implement Working Hours Compliance Check (45-Hour Weekly Limit)
Sabah/Sarawak Labour Ordinance 2025 (Acts A1753/A1754) â€” Extend Payroll Statutory Protections to All East Malaysia Employees Regardless of Salary

userStories.title: Sabah/Sarawak Labour Ordinance 2025 â€” RM50,000 Penalty Exposure Alert for East Malaysia Wage and Overtime Violations
userStories.description: Acts A1753 and A1754 increased the maximum penalty for labour violations (underpayment, late wages, overtime denial, unlawful deduction) in Sabah and Sarawak from RM10,000 to RM50,000 â€” a five-fold increase. Existing compliance alerts display a generic penalty or the Peninsular Malaysia RM10,000/RM20,000 scale. HR managers overseeing East Malaysia employees need violation alerts that explicitly show the RM50,000 maximum exposure specific to the applicable ordinance, so they can prioritise remediation proportionately.
acceptanceCriteria: Compliance violation alerts for Sabah/Sarawak jurisdiction-flagged employees display RM50,000 as the maximum penalty amount (not RM10,000 or RM20,000)
The penalty quantum shown references the correct ordinance: 'Sabah Labour Ordinance (Amendment) Act 2025' or 'Sarawak Labour Ordinance (Amendment) Act 2025'
Monthly compliance risk summary report for East Malaysia employees includes aggregate maximum penalty exposure based on violation count Ã— RM50,000
Alert severity for East Malaysia violations is set to Critical for any flagged underpayment or overtime denial given the higher penalty quantum
Penalty quantum is configurable in an Ordinance Penalty Settings table to allow future gazette updates without code changes
technicalNotes: RM50,000 penalty effective 1 May 2025 under both Act A1753 (Sabah) and Act A1754 (Sarawak)
Existing 'Minimum Wage Enforcement â€” Penalty Quantum Awareness Panel' covers Peninsular rules â€” extend with East Malaysia ordinance penalty tier
This is a compliance UI/alerting enhancement; the violation detection logic is in the extended-coverage story above
dependencies: Add Sabah/Sarawak Labour Jurisdiction Flag on Employee
Sabah/Sarawak Labour Ordinance 2025 (Acts A1753/A1754) â€” Extend Payroll Statutory Protections to All East Malaysia Employees Regardless of Salary
Minimum Wage Enforcement â€” Penalty Quantum Awareness Panel and Ongoing Violation Daily Fine Tracker

userStories.title: Gig Workers Act 872 â€” Generate PERKESO SESSS Contribution Deduction Statement for Each Platform Worker Payment
userStories.description: Act 872 requires platform providers to deduct PERKESO SESSS contributions from gig worker earnings and provide the worker with a statement showing the deduction at each payment event. This is a new document type â€” distinct from an employment payslip (gig workers are not employees). The existing story 'Gig Workers Act 2025 â€” SKSPS Per-Transaction Contribution Calculation and PERKESO Monthly Aggregate Remittance' covers the calculation and monthly remittance but not the per-payment statement that must be issued to the individual gig worker.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Platform Worker Payment Statement' is generated for each payment to a gig worker, showing: gross payment amount, PERKESO SESSS deduction amount and scheme code, net payment to worker, PERKESO registration number, and contribution period
Statements are linkable to the monthly PERKESO aggregate remittance file for reconciliation
Statements are available for the gig worker to download (email or portal link)
System warns if a statement is generated for a worker not yet registered with PERKESO SESSS, blocking issuance until registration is confirmed
All generated statements are stored and searchable by worker, payment date, and contribution period
technicalNotes: Enforcement date pending ministerial gazette â€” system should queue statements but hold delivery until enforcement date per the enforcement-gate story
PERKESO SESSS contribution rate and deduction basis (% of earnings per transaction) to be confirmed from PERKESO gazette when published
This is NOT a payslip under EA Section 25A; it is a contractual payment statement under Act 872
Contribution statements tie back to the monthly PERKESO LINDUNG aggregate upload
dependencies: Gig Workers Act 2025 â€” SKSPS Per-Transaction Contribution Calculation and PERKESO Monthly Aggregate Remittance
Gig Workers Act 872 â€” Enforce PERKESO Deduction on Separate Ministerial Gazette Enforcement Date, Not Royal Assent

userStories.title: MyInvois Phase 4 Relaxation Period Expiry Engine â€” Alert and Auto-Transition to Individual Self-Billed e-Invoice from 1 January 2027
userStories.description: LHDN extended the Phase 4 e-invoice relaxation period for companies with annual revenue RM1Mâ€“RM5M through 31 December 2026 (full year). During this period, consolidated e-invoices are permitted for sub-RM10,000 payroll expense reimbursements. From 1 January 2027, the relaxation expires and all transactions require individual e-invoices. No existing story implements the expiry logic or the transition workflow. Without this, Phase 4 employers will inadvertently continue issuing invalid consolidated e-invoices in 2027, risking LHDN penalties.
acceptanceCriteria: Phase 4 companies display their relaxation expiry date (31 December 2026) on the LHDN/MyInvois settings page
An alert fires 90 days before expiry (1 October 2026) prompting finance to prepare for full individual e-invoice issuance

From 1 January 2027, the system no longer allows consolidated self-billed e-invoice generation for Phase 4 companies â€” all transactions including sub-RM10,000 expense reimbursements must generate individual self-billed e-invoices
A compliance readiness checklist is generated at the 90-day mark listing any payroll expense reimbursement workflows still relying on consolidated e-invoice logic
Any attempt to generate a consolidated self-billed e-invoice for a Phase 4 company after 31 December 2026 is blocked with a clear error message citing the expired relaxation period
technicalNotes: LHDN extended relaxation to 31 December 2026 â€” the full year, not the original 6 months
During relaxation: consolidated e-invoices permitted, individual required only for single transactions > RM10,000
After relaxation: individual self-billed e-invoices required for all payroll expense reimbursements and perquisites
Add a relaxation_expiry_date field on the Company doctype alongside the existing Phase 4 e-invoice flag
dependencies: MyInvois Phase 4 (January 2026) â€” Set Mandatory e-Invoice Flag for Companies with RM1Mâ€“RM5M Annual Revenue
Self-Billed e-Invoice for Employee Expense Claims and Perquisite Reimbursements (MyInvois Phase 4)
MyInvois RM10,000 Non-Consolidatable Transaction Rule â€” Route Large Payroll Expense Reimbursements to Individual Self-Billed e-Invoice

userStories.title: Progressive Wage Policy 2026 Training Hours Carry-Forward â€” Track 42-Hour Accumulated Minimum for Employers with 2025 Shortfall
userStories.description: Malaysia's Progressive Wage Policy (PWP) requires each eligible employee to complete 21 hours of approved training annually. For 2026, the Ministry confirmed that training hours not completed in 2025 carry forward, meaning affected employers must ensure a minimum of up to 42 cumulative training hours in 2026. The existing story 'Progressive Wage Policy â€” 21-Hour Annual Training Completion Tracking per Eligible Employee' tracks the annual 21-hour target but does not implement the carry-forward mechanism or the elevated 2026 minimum, risking loss of PWP incentive payments for employers with a 2025 shortfall.
acceptanceCriteria: For each PWP-eligible employee, the system reads 2025 training hours completed and calculates the carry-forward shortfall (maximum 21 hours)
The 2026 training hours target for each employee is set to 21 + carry-forward shortfall, capped at 42 hours total
A per-employee dashboard shows: 2025 hours completed, shortfall carried forward, 2026 target, 2026 hours completed to date, and remaining hours
Alerts fire when a PWP-eligible employee is within 60 days of the December 2026 deadline with insufficient training hours remaining
Carry-forward amount is zero for employees who completed the full 21 hours in 2025 â€” their 2026 target remains 21 hours
PWP incentive claim export includes per-employee training hours with carry-forward breakdown for submission evidence
technicalNotes: Source: 'Training requirements of 21 hours that were not completed in 2025 can carry forward until December 2026, allowing employees to complete a minimum of 42 hours in 2026'
Integration with HRD Corp eTRIS for pulling completed training hours per employee per year
PWP incentive payment (RM300/employee/month for 12 months) requires evidence of salary increment AND training hour completion
Monitor mohr.gov.my and SOCSO/JTK for updated PWP guidelines if carry-forward rules change
dependencies: Progressive Wage Policy â€” 21-Hour Annual Training Completion Tracking per Eligible Employee
HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard

userStories.title: e-Duti Setem STSDS â€” Employer Self-Assessment Stamp Duty Declaration Generator for Employment Contracts
userStories.description: From 1 January 2026, LHDN replaced the STAMPS portal with e-Duti Setem (accessible via MyTax), implementing the Self-Assessment System for Stamp Duty (STSDS). Under STSDS, employers must now self-calculate and self-declare stamp duty on employment contracts (RM10/contract) without prior LHDN officer assessment. The RM3,000/month wage exemption threshold (Finance Act 2025) also changed from the old RM300/month. The existing stories 'Track Employment Contract Stamp Duty Compliance via e-Duti Setem MyTax' and 'e-Duti Setem Per-Employee Contract Stamp Duty Compliance Task with 30-Day Countdown' track deadlines but do not generate the self-assessment declaration form data required for STSDS submission.
acceptanceCriteria: For each employment contract marked as executed, the system automatically calculates stamp duty payable: RM10 for contracts where employee salary exceeds RM3,000/month; nil for salary at or below RM3,000/month (Finance Act 2025 exemption effective 1 January 2026)

A self-assessment declaration record is generated per contract, containing: contract execution date, employee name, salary, stamp duty calculated, exemption basis (if nil), and the employer's self-assessment affirmation
The declaration data is exportable in a format compatible with e-Duti Setem STSDS submission on MyTax (format to be confirmed from hasil.gov.my/duti-setem/e-duti-setem/)
An audit trail of all STSDS declarations is maintained: submission date, e-Duti Setem acknowledgement reference (once received), and status (Draft / Submitted / Acknowledged)
Alert fires if a contract passes 30 days from execution without a completed and submitted STSDS declaration
technicalNotes: RM3,000/month exemption: Finance Act 2025, effective 1 January 2026 (previously RM300/month threshold)
No penalty during grace period 1 January 2026 â€“ 31 December 2026 for STSDS; penalties resume 1 January 2027
Access e-Duti Setem via MyTax portal (same login as employer e-PCB Plus, e-CP22 accounts)
KPMG e-Duti Setem guide December 2025 is the most current implementation reference
dependencies: Track Employment Contract Stamp Duty Compliance via e-Duti Setem MyTax (Mandatory from 2026)
e-Duti Setem Per-Employee Contract Stamp Duty Compliance Task with 30-Day Countdown (Effective January 2026)
Update Employment Contract Stamp Duty Exemption Threshold to RM3,000 Monthly Wage (Budget 2026)
